  • Implement scheduling and orchestration patterns (Airflow/Prefect) and monitor pipelines with data-quality checks and alerting.
  • Apply partitioning, clustering and compression to optimize query performance and storage costs.
Prerequisites

Comfortable with Python, basic SQL and familiarity with cloud storage concepts. Some exposure to Spark or a similar distributed engine is helpful but not strictly required.

Duration

10–16 hours of guided labs and a final mini-project implementing an end-to-end pipeline.

Difficulty

Intermediate → Advanced (practical engineering emphasis)

Learning outcomes
  • Implement repeatable ETL jobs that can be run locally and in cloud environments.
  • Choose storage formats and compression schemes appropriate for analytics workloads.
  • Design partitioning and bucketing strategies to speed queries while controlling storage overhead.
  • Build simple orchestration workflows, add unit/integration tests for ETL steps and instrument pipelines for monitoring and alerting.
  • Document data contracts, lineage and operational runbooks to support reliable handoffs between teams.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Data storage options: object stores, data warehouses, columnar formats and trade-offs (latency, cost, schema evolution).
  2. IO and serialization: CSV pitfalls, parquet/arrow advantages, compression choices and handling nested schemas.
  3. Partitioning & layout: date/partition design, file sizes, small-file problems and compaction strategies.
  4. Processing patterns: batch vs incremental vs streaming; watermarks, exactly-once vs at-least-once semantics, and checkpointing basics.
  5. Orchestration: DAG design, task dependencies, retries, backfills, and parameterization (Airflow/Prefect patterns).
  6. Data quality & testing: schema validation, row-level checks, assertions, and data drift monitoring.
  7. Operational considerations: logging/observability, cost control, security (IAM, encryption) and governance (lineage, provenance).
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Ingest raw files into an object store; detect schema differences and normalize incoming event payloads.
  2. Design and implement a canonical schema and write conversion functions that coerce and validate fields.
  3. Implement incremental transforms that process only new partitions, produce aggregated feature tables and write partitioned parquet outputs.
  4. Create Airflow/Prefect DAG to orchestrate ingestion → transform → publish steps, include retries and backfills.
  5. Add unit/integration tests for transformation logic, plus row-level data checks and a validation report step in the DAG.
  6. Instrument metrics (run time, row counts, error rates) and build a simple dashboard/alert for failed runs and anomalous row-count changes.
Example
# pseudocode: incremental parquet write
new_files = list_new_raw_files(s3://bucket/raw/)
for f in new_files:
  df = load_csv(f)
  df = coerce_schema(df, schema)
  df = clean_and_enrich(df)
  df.to_parquet('s3://bucket/processed/date=2025-01-01/', partition_cols=['date'])
Assessment

Deliverables: a repository containing (1) ETL transformation code and tests, (2) an orchestrator DAG to run the pipeline, (3) a README describing the canonical schema and deployment instructions, and (4) a short runbook describing failure modes, monitoring metrics and a rollback/playbook.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Design idempotent transformation steps so retries are safe—use atomic writes and temporary staging directories.
  • Small files kill performance—target consistent file sizes (e.g., 128–512MB) and plan compaction strategies.
  • Prefer explicit schema declarations and automated checks over relying on schema inference.
  • Measure cost and latency trade-offs early: real-world data grows fast—plan retention and lifecycle policies.
Resources
  • Guides: Apache Parquet/Arrow docs, Airflow and Prefect patterns, cloud storage best practices, and example ETL repos from practitioners (dbt, spark-based examples).
ds-featurestore:
Overview

Feature stores centralize feature definitions, storage and serving so machine learning teams can reuse, monitor and serve high-quality features consistently between training and production. This module explains the architectural patterns and practical engineering needed to design and operate a feature store: offline (training) stores, online (serving) stores, feature transformations, versioning, backfills and consistency guarantees. You will learn how feature stores reduce duplication, prevent training/serving skew, and accelerate model development through standardized feature contracts and lineage.

Objectives
  • Understand feature store architecture: offline vs online, join keys, time-travel and freshness guarantees.
  • Design feature tables with clear schemas, semantics and freshness constraints for safe joins in training and serving.
  • Implement transform logic that is reusable in both batch training and low-latency serving environments.
  • Establish feature versioning, lineage tracking and monitoring for feature quality and drift.
Prerequisites

Experience with pandas or Spark transformations, familiarity with SQL, basic understanding of ML training pipelines and production serving considerations.

Duration

8–14 hours including labs that implement a simple feature table and an online lookup simulator.

Difficulty

Intermediate → Advanced (systems + ML engineering)

Learning outcomes
  • Define and implement offline feature computation logic and materialize features for training with point-in-time correctness.
  • Design an online feature API for low-latency lookups and consistency checks between offline and online stores.
  • Implement feature versioning and lineage so features can be audited and rolled back.
  • Monitor feature distributions, freshness and completeness and trigger alerts for drift or quality regressions.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Feature store concepts: offline vs online stores, entity keys, point-in-time correctness and backfill strategies.
  2. Transform portability: writing transformations that run in both batch (Spark/pandas) and serving contexts.
  3. Materialization & storage: parquet-based offline tables, online key-value stores (Redis/Dynamo) and cache warmup patterns.
  4. Versioning & lineage: feature schema versioning, change management and metadata tracking for reproducibility.
  5. Serving patterns: batch joins for training, feature lookup APIs for inference, and hybrid caching approaches.
  6. Monitoring & governance: feature drift detection, SLA for freshness, and ownership/metadata best practices.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Design a simple entity schema (user_id, event_time) and implement a set of feature transforms (aggregates, time-based stats).
  2. Compute and materialize offline features with point-in-time joins for training datasets and store them as partitioned parquet tables.
  3. Implement a lightweight online store simulator: load selective feature slices into a key-value store and expose a REST lookup API.
  4. Create tests that verify offline and online feature equivalence for a sample of keys and times (prevent training/serving skew).
  5. Add monitoring: compute and plot feature distribution summaries, freshness metrics, and implement alerts for missing features or stale values.
Example
# pseudo-example: offline aggregate for feature store
features = events.groupby('user_id').rolling('7d', on='event_time').agg({'event_value':'mean'})
features.to_parquet('s3://features/user_weekly_avg/', partition_cols=['date'])
# online lookup: key-value (user_id -> latest feature)
Assessment

Deliver a small feature-store prototype: (1) offline feature computation scripts and partitioned outputs, (2) an online lookup API or simulator, (3) tests verifying point-in-time correctness between offline and online stores, and (4) a monitoring dashboard and brief runbook describing data freshness and ownership.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Point-in-time correctness is critical—ensure training joins never use future information.
  • Keep transforms idempotent and testable; avoid ad-hoc per-model transforms that diverge in production.
  • Plan for cardinality and scale in online stores—high-cardinality features may not be suited for low-latency stores without caching strategies.
  • Document feature semantics and ownership; metadata is crucial for debugging and trust.
Resources
  • Feature store projects and patterns (Feast, Tecton), docs on serving architectures, parity-testing recipes and monitoring strategies for features in production.
da-ab:
Overview

A/B Testing (randomized experiments) is a rigorous, pragmatic framework for learning what changes actually move user behavior and business metrics. This module provides an end-to-end, practice-first guide: how to formulate clear hypotheses and estimands, choose and instrument reliable metrics, plan sample sizes and test duration, run experiments safely (ramping/canarying and monitoring), analyze results with appropriate statistical tools and guardrails, and translate findings into operational decisions. The emphasis is on reproducibility, measurement fidelity and aligning statistical outcomes with business value so experiments inform high-quality, low-regret decisions.

Objectives
  • Define crisp experimental questions and estimands (ATE, conditional ATE) connected to business outcomes.
  • Compute required sample sizes and design safe stopping and monitoring rules to avoid biased inference.
  • Instrument experiments with high-quality metrics and reconciliation checks to ensure data fidelity.
  • Analyze results using sound statistical methods and produce decision-oriented reports that weigh uncertainty, risk and opportunity.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with basic statistics (means, variance, hypothesis testing), comfort with Python or R for analysis, and working knowledge of product metrics (DAU, conversion, retention). Some exposure to analytics or event-tracking systems is helpful.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours of guided lessons, simulations and a short experimental design + analysis project.

Learning outcomes
  • Write clear experiment specs including hypothesis, primary metric, guardrails, randomization unit and analysis plan.
  • Perform sample-size / power calculations that account for real-world variance and business minimum detectable effect (MDE).
  • Implement monitoring dashboards and automated checks that surface instrumentation problems (missing events, bucketing issues, data loss).
  • Analyze experimental outcomes with appropriate confidence intervals, corrections for multiple comparisons and robust alternatives (bootstrap, permutation) when assumptions fail.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Foundations: estimands vs estimators, units of randomization (user, session, device), and why clear estimands matter for causal claims.
  2. Metric design: picking primary vs guardrail metrics, variance-stabilizing transforms (log, arcsine), and practical considerations for ratio or rate metrics (denominators, denominators stability).
  3. Power & sample size: computing MDEs given baseline rates/variance, trade-offs between sensitivity and test duration, handling seasonality and user heterogeneity in calculations.
  4. Randomization & instrumentation: deterministic bucketing, cookie/ID-based assignment, dealing with cross-device users and reconciliation patterns between event and experiment pipelines.
  5. Sequential testing & stopping: risks of optional stopping, alpha-spending and group-sequential designs, practical monitoring strategies and safe thresholds for decision-making.
  6. Analysis techniques: difference-in-means, t-tests with robust SEs, bootstrap CI, permutation tests, adjustments for multiple metrics (FWER vs FDR) and pre-specified hierarchical testing.
  7. Heterogeneous effects: pre-specified subgroup analysis, interaction tests, issues of multiplicity and the need for replication/validation for discovered segments.
  8. Operationalization: ramp plans, canary rollouts, rollback criteria, and post-launch monitoring for sustained effect and regression.
  9. Governance: experiment registry, pre-analysis plans, documentation, experiment lifecycle (design → run → analyze → decision → archive) and ethical considerations for experiments on users.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Define an experiment: state the hypothesis, primary metric, estimand (ATE), randomization unit and sample-size calculation for a realistic MDE.
  2. Simulate experimental data under null and alternative scenarios to validate power calculations and illustrate Type I/II trade-offs.
  3. Create an analysis notebook: compute point estimates, bootstrap confidence intervals, p-values and an uncertainty-aware business impact estimate (expected monetary value of the change).
  4. Implement simple monitoring checks: sample-rate stability, event-loss detection and balance checks across treatment groups; demonstrate how to surface failures to engineers.
  5. Draft a decision memo: concise summary of results, uncertainty, recommended action (promote, iterate, kill), and operational next steps (monitoring, rollout schedule).
Example
# sample power calc (python pseudocode)
from statsmodels.stats.power import TTestIndPower
analysis = TTestIndPower()
# baseline conversion 0.08, MDE absolute 0.01, alpha 0.05, power 0.8
n = analysis.solve_power(effect_size=0.01/0.08, power=0.8, alpha=0.05, alternative='two-sided')
print('per-group sample size approx:', int(n))
# simulate and analyze with bootstrap/p-value adjustments as needed
Assessment

Deliver a reproducible notebook containing: (1) a formal experiment spec (estimand, metric, sample-size calc, stopping rules); (2) simulation validating power/sensitivity choices; (3) analysis of a synthetic or real experiment with CI and business-impact translation; and (4) a short decision memo recommending next steps and operational rollout plan.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Pre-register primary metric and analysis plan—post-hoc metric fishing biases results and undermines trust.
  • Instrument metrics end-to-end and reconcile event streams; most experiment failures come from telemetry bugs, not statistics.
  • Be conservative with early stopping—optional peeking inflates false positives unless properly corrected.
  • Treat subgroup findings as exploratory unless pre-specified or replicated; many spurious segments appear by chance.
  • Translate statistics into business terms: report expected value, risk and decision rule—not just p-values.
da-cleaning:
Overview

Data Cleaning is the essential practice of transforming raw, often messy, datasets into a reliable, analysable form. This topic covers practical strategies and engineering patterns to find, diagnose, and correct common quality problems — missing values, inconsistent types, duplicate records, malformed text, timezone errors and outliers — while preserving provenance and reproducibility. The emphasis is on building defensible rules that can be automated and tested so analysts and engineers can trust downstream analyses and machine learning models. Beyond tactical fixes, learners will develop a principled decision-making process to choose appropriate remediation for different kinds of defects and how to measure the impact of cleaning on key metrics.

Objectives
  • Understand common sources of data problems and how they manifest in real datasets.
  • Learn systematic inspection techniques to prioritize cleaning tasks based on business impact.
  • Implement robust cleaning operations that are deterministic, testable and reversible when appropriate.
  • Produce reproducible artifacts including cleaned datasets, validation reports and transformation code.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with Python and pandas (or equivalent tooling), basic SQL, and elementary statistics. Comfort reading and editing Jupyter notebooks is helpful.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of guided content and practical exercises, plus an optional project to consolidate skills.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Design ingestion pipelines that coerce heterogeneous sources into a canonical schema.
  • Detect and categorize missingness patterns and apply principled imputation or flagging strategies.
  • Normalize categorical values, standardize units and handle timestamp/timezone issues safely.
  • Create validation checks and lightweight tests to prevent regressions in data quality.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Ingestion and schema contracts: safe reading of CSV/JSON/Parquet, explicit dtypes, parsing dates and handling encoding differences.
  2. Exploratory diagnostics: automated summaries of nulls, unique value counts, distributional checks, cardinality profiling and quick visual checks for anomalies.
  3. Missing data taxonomy and strategy: identify MCAR, MAR and MNAR patterns and choose between dropping, imputing, or treating missing as a feature; compare simple imputation against model-based approaches.
  4. Type coercion and normalization: coerce numeric and datetime types, standardize categorical text using mapping tables, normalize units and currency, and correct common parsing errors.
  5. Duplicate detection and canonical keys: identify near-duplicates, merge strategies, and defining stable entity keys for downstream joins.
  6. Outlier detection and handling: robust statistics, winsorization, clipping and applying domain-aware rules that preserve meaningful extreme values where relevant.
  7. Provenance, reproducibility and auditing: log transformations, save intermediate snapshots, use deterministic seeds, and create a validation report documenting each step and rationale.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Load a multi-source dataset and create a canonical schema function that enforces dtypes and required fields.
  2. Produce a data-quality dashboard: column-level null percentages, cardinality, sample invalid rows and histogram summaries for numeric fields.
  3. Implement cleaning routines: text normalization mappings, timestamp normalization to UTC, and group-wise imputation for missing values where appropriate.
  4. Design and run validation checks after cleaning: row counts by partition, unique key checks, acceptable null thresholds and sample-based equality checks against a gold snapshot.
  5. Export cleaned outputs as partitioned Parquet and include a short README and JSON manifest describing transformations and schema.
Example
# pseudocode for a canonical ingestion function
import pandas as pd

def coerce_schema(df):
    df['event_time'] = pd.to_datetime(df['event_time'], errors='coerce')
    df['user_id'] = pd.to_numeric(df['user_id'], errors='coerce').astype('Int64')
    df['country'] = df['country'].str.strip().str.lower().replace({'united states':'us','usa':'us'})
    return df
Assessment

Submit a reproducible notebook that ingests raw files, applies a canonical schema, runs cleaning transforms and produces a validation report. The deliverable should include: the cleaned dataset (sample or partitioned Parquet), transformation code, a README documenting assumptions and a short section measuring how cleaning changed key aggregates and sample-level records to demonstrate impact.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Prefer small, reversible steps and keep raw snapshots; irreversible changes complicate audits and debugging.
  • Donâ€™t drop rows blindly; document why rows were removed and quantify the effect on aggregates and representativeness.
  • When imputing values, validate against holdout samples and measure downstream impact on metrics or model performance.
  • Use explicit dtype declarations instead of relying on inference to avoid platform-dependent surprises.
Resources
  • Recommended readings and tools: pandas documentation, data validation libraries (pandera, great expectations), canonical schema patterns, and articles on missing data handling and defensive ETL practices.
da-causal:
Overview

Causal inference is the set of conceptual and practical tools used to move from correlation to actionable claims about cause and effect in data analysis. In applied data work, causal thinking clarifies the estimand of interest, the assumptions required to identify that estimand, and the design and analysis choices that make conclusions defensible. This topic teaches practitioners how to formulate causal questions, select appropriate identification strategies, implement estimation methods that are robust to common violations, and communicate causal conclusions with appropriate caveats. The emphasis is on pragmatic, reproducible workflows that combine domain knowledge with statistical tools to support decisions, policy experiments, or product interventions.

Objectives
  • Learn how to translate business or scientific questions into causal estimands and causal graphs.
  • Understand common identification strategies such as randomized experiments, instrumental variables, difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and propensity score methods.
  • Implement estimation and sensitivity analyses that probe identifying assumptions and quantify robustness.
  • Communicate causal findings clearly, including assumptions, limitations and recommended next steps for validation or deployment.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with basic statistics and regression, comfort with Python or R, and experience analyzing tabular datasets. Prior exposure to experimental design is helpful but not required.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours of guided lessons and practical labs, plus optional exercises to apply methods to case studies.

Learning outcomes
  • Formulate precise causal estimands and represent assumed data generating processes using causal diagrams.
  • Choose and implement a valid identification strategy for a given data context and assess the plausibility of required assumptions.
  • Apply estimation methods and conduct sensitivity checks to evaluate how violations of assumptions may affect conclusions.
  • Produce a concise causal analysis report that includes estimand, design, diagnostics and a clear statement of the strength of evidence.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Foundations: potential outcomes, causal graphs, treatment assignment mechanisms and the importance of clearly stated estimands.
  2. Randomized experiments: design, stratification, blocking, analysis strategies and handling noncompliance with intent-to-treat and complier average causal effect estimators.
  3. Observational strategies: matching and propensity scores, inverse probability weighting, doubly robust estimators and practical diagnostics for balance and overlap.
  4. Quasi-experimental designs: difference-in-differences, regression discontinuity, and instrumental variables — when each is appropriate and how to implement them carefully.
  5. Sensitivity analysis and robustness: bounding approaches, placebo tests, negative controls and quantitative sensitivity measures for unobserved confounding.
  6. Heterogeneous treatment effects: estimating conditional average treatment effects, subgroup analysis best practices and pitfalls with multiple comparisons.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Translate a business question into a causal estimand and draw a simple causal diagram to encode assumptions.
  2. If an experiment is available, analyze it with pre-specified estimands and check randomization balance; otherwise, select an observational identification strategy and justify its validity.
  3. Implement estimation (matching, IPW, DiD, IV or RD) and run diagnostics: covariate balance, overlap, falsification/placebo tests and sensitivity analyses for unobserved confounding.
  4. Estimate average and heterogenous effects, visualize treatment effect distributions and summarize findings for non-technical stakeholders with clear assumption statements.
Example
# pseudocode for a propensity score workflow
ps = LogisticRegression().fit(X_covariates, treatment)
weights = treatment/ps.predict_proba(X_covariates)[:,1] + (1-treatment)/(1-ps.predict_proba(X_covariates)[:,1])
ate = weighted_mean(outcome, weights, treatment)
Assessment

Deliver a notebook containing: a clear estimand, a causal diagram, chosen identification strategy with justification, estimation code with diagnostics and sensitivity checks, and a concise write-up that translates statistical results into business or policy recommendations. Include discussions of residual uncertainty and suggested validation steps such as randomized follow-ups or alternative quasi-experimental checks.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Always begin with a clearly stated estimand; many disagreements stem from ambiguous targets rather than data analysis errors.
  • Document assumptions explicitly and perform falsification tests where possible; untested assumptions are the weakest point of causal claims.
  • Avoid over-interpreting subgroup analyses unless pre-specified or validated by replication; treat exploratory heterogeneity as hypothesis-generating.
  • Translate causal findings into actionable decisions by including expected benefits, uncertainties and required operational changes to realize estimated effects.
Resources
  • Recommended readings and tools: Causal Inference textbooks and primers, DoWhy and econml libraries, applied case studies in policy and product analytics, and literature on sensitivity analysis and design-based inference.
da-reporting:
Overview

Reporting and dashboards are how analytical insight enters operational decision-making. This topic covers the design, engineering and governance of reports and dashboards that inform teams reliably and efficiently. Good reporting is more than visual polish; it requires careful definition of metrics, reproducible data pipelines, thoughtful UX for interpretation, and monitoring to ensure metrics remain valid over time. This module teaches practitioners how to design metrics with clear definitions, build reproducible back-end pipelines, craft dashboards that support both exploration and operational monitoring, and establish practices for versioning, ownership and alerting so reports remain trustworthy as systems evolve.

Objectives
  • Define and implement canonical metrics with unambiguous calculation rules and edge case handling.
  • Design dashboards that highlight key decisions, enable rapid root-cause analysis and support both high-level and drill-down views.
  • Build reproducible pipelines for metric computation and dashboard refresh, including testing, monitoring and data lineage.
  • Establish governance: metric ownership, a living metric catalog, change management and alerting best practices.
Prerequisites

Experience with SQL for analytics, familiarity with at least one visualization or dashboarding tool (Looker, Tableau, PowerBI, Superset, or similar), and understanding of upstream data schemas and pipelines.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of lessons and labs plus a practical dashboard project to consolidate learning.

Learning outcomes
  • Create a metric specification document that unambiguously defines primary KPIs and guardrail metrics, including denominators, filtering rules and edge cases.
  • Implement a reproducible ETL flow or SQL-based materialization process for metrics with automated tests and deployment processes.
  • Design dashboard layouts that prioritize clarity: topline KPIs, trend lines with context, segmentation widgets and anomaly indicators.
  • Instrument dashboards with monitoring and alerts for data freshness, metric drift and reconciliation breaks and provide a playbook for triage and remediation.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Metric design: canonical definitions, handling duplicates and late-arriving events, time-alignment rules and denominator choices for rate metrics.
  2. Data pipelines: materializing metric tables, incremental computation, cache strategies and reproducible SQL/ELT workflows.
  3. Dashboard UX: storytelling with visuals, using small multiples and sparklines, choosing color and annotation for clarity, and balancing interactivity with performance.
  4. Testing and validation: row-level tests, reconciliation tests, end-to-end smoke tests and alerting for freshness or aggregate divergences.
  5. Governance and lifecycle: metric cataloging, changelogs, owner assignment and a review process for metric changes and dashboard releases.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Choose a core business question and define a primary KPI with exact SQL or pseudocode, including treatment of edge cases.
  2. Implement a reproducible metric computation pipeline that writes partitioned outputs and include tests that assert expected row counts and values on sample inputs.
  3. Design a dashboard wireframe and implement it in a chosen tool; include filters, drill-downs and a one-page executive view plus a technical diagnostics tab for engineers.
  4. Add monitoring: reconciliation jobs that compare nightly aggregates against source totals, alerts for missing partitions and a simple incident runbook for metric anomalies.
  5. Document the dashboard: metric definitions, owner contacts, refresh cadence and a brief guide on interpreting each chart and recommended actions for common signals.
Example
# pseudocode: daily active users materialization
WITH events AS (
  SELECT user_id, date_trunc('day', event_time) AS date
  FROM raw_events WHERE event_time >= current_date - interval '30 days'
)
SELECT date, count(distinct user_id) AS dau
FROM events GROUP BY date ORDER BY date;
Assessment

Deliver a dashboard project including: a metric specification document, reproducible metric computation code or SQL, the implemented dashboard with an executive and a diagnostics view, monitoring for freshness and reconciliation, and a short README describing ownership, expected usage and change management procedures.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Prioritize metric clarity over novelty; ambiguous metrics undermine cross-team alignment.
  • Automate tests and reconciliation checks early; most reporting errors are pipeline or schema regressions, not visualization bugs.
  • Keep executive views simple and reserve complexity for technical diagnostic tabs intended for engineers.
  • Document everything: metric definitions, data lineage, owners and the expected cadence for reviews and audits.
Resources
  • Recommended readings and tools: dashboarding UX guides, data catalogs and metric registry patterns, testing frameworks for analytics pipelines, and example dashboards with annotated interpretation guides.
da-statmodel:
Overview

Statistical Modeling (applied) is the bridge between raw data summaries and reliable, actionable inference or prediction. This module focuses on pragmatic model building with an emphasis on interpretation, diagnostics, quantifying uncertainty and communicating results to stakeholders. It covers classical linear methods, generalized linear models, flexible semi-parametric approaches, model validation for predictive tasks, and a compact introduction to hierarchical models. The goal is to equip practitioners to choose models appropriate to their objective (explanation versus prediction), check assumptions, quantify uncertainty correctly, and produce concise, defensible writeups that inform decisions.

Objectives
  • Understand the distinction between estimation for interpretation and modeling for prediction, and choose evaluation strategies accordingly.
  • Fit linear and generalized linear models, interpret coefficients on the response and link scales, and compute robust standard errors when needed.
  • Detect misspecification with diagnostics (residuals, leverage, influence) and apply remedies such as transformations, splines or alternative families.
  • Apply model selection and regularization judiciously, and communicate model uncertainty with intervals and predictive distributions.
Prerequisites

Comfort with basic calculus and linear algebra, familiarity with regression concepts, and working knowledge of Python or R for model fitting and plotting.

Duration

Approximately 10–14 hours of lessons and practical labs, culminating in a short modeling report.

Learning outcomes
  • Fit and interpret ordinary least squares models and common GLM families (Gaussian, Binomial, Poisson), and understand when each is appropriate.
  • Diagnose and remedy common assumption violations: heteroscedasticity, nonlinearity, influential observations and correlated errors.
  • Use splines or generalized additive models to flexibly model nonlinear effects while retaining interpretability.
  • Quantify predictive performance with cross-validation, report calibration and produce predictive intervals for new observations.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Linear regression review: geometry of least squares, interpretation of coefficients, confidence intervals and hypothesis tests.
  2. Diagnostics and robustness: residuals vs fitted, QQ plots, tests for heteroscedasticity, leverage and Cook's distance, and robust standard errors.
  3. GLMs: link functions, interpretation on link vs response scales, overdispersion and quasi-likelihood remedies for count and binary data.
  4. Nonlinearity and splines: piecewise polynomials, natural splines and smoothing parameter selection; introduction to GAMs for interpretable nonlinearity.
  5. Regularization and model selection: AIC/BIC, cross-validation, Ridge and Lasso penalties and trade-offs for prediction vs interpretability.
  6. Hierarchical models overview: partial pooling, varying intercepts/slopes and when hierarchical structure improves estimates.
  7. Prediction vs inference: nested CV for hyperparameter tuning, calibration checks, and reporting predictive intervals with appropriate coverage.
  8. Communicating results: concise coefficient tables, effect plots, marginal effects at representative values, and plain-language uncertainty interpretation.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Fit a baseline linear model on a real tabular dataset and produce a standard coefficient table with confidence intervals; interpret top predictors.
  2. Run diagnostic plots, identify violations and implement remedies (log or Box-Cox transforms, robust SEs, or GLM family changes).
  3. Introduce nonlinearity using splines or a GAM for a key predictor and visualize the smooth effect with confidence bands.
  4. Compare predictive performance between OLS, a regularized regression and a flexible tree-based model using nested cross-validation; report both predictive metrics and interpretability trade-offs.
  5. Write a short modeling memo that states the estimand, model choices, diagnostics, predictive validation and recommended next steps for production or further study.
Example
# example (python pseudocode using statsmodels and patsy)
import statsmodels.formula.api as smf
model = smf.ols('outcome ~ age + C(region) + np.log(income)', data=df).fit()
print(model.summary())
# diagnostics
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
plt.scatter(model.fittedvalues, model.resid)
Assessment

Submit a reproducible notebook and a two-page write-up: (1) clear modeling goal and estimand; (2) model specification and rationale; (3) diagnostics and remedial steps; (4) validation results (CV or holdout) with predictive intervals; and (5) a plain-English summary of implications, uncertainties and recommended actions.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Always be explicit whether the model is intended for causal interpretation or prediction; validation and reporting differ markedly by goal.
  • Report effect sizes with confidence or credible intervals rather than relying only on p-values; emphasize practical significance.
  • Complex models can mask misspecification—use simpler baselines as sanity checks and replicate key findings under alternative specifications.
  • Quantify predictive uncertainty and check calibration; a well-calibrated predictive interval is often more useful operationally than a slightly better point estimate.
web-htmlcss:
Overview

HTML & CSS form the foundation of web development: HTML provides the structure and semantic meaning of content, while CSS controls presentation, layout and responsive behavior. This module takes a pragmatic, hands-on approach to teaching both languages in tandem so you can build modern, robust, accessible and maintainable user interfaces. You will learn semantic markup, modern layout systems (Flexbox and Grid), responsive design patterns, CSS architecture and best practices for performance and maintainability. The course balances beginner-friendly fundamentals (elements, attributes, box model) with practical engineering topics (accessibility, scalability, tooling and build pipelines) so you can deliver production-ready front-end code.

Objectives
  • Master semantic HTML to improve accessibility, SEO and maintainability.
  • Use modern CSS layout techniques (Flexbox, Grid) to create responsive, robust layouts.
  • Implement responsive design patterns, breakpoints and fluid typography for multi-device experiences.
  • Adopt CSS organization strategies (BEM, utility classes, component-based CSS) and tooling (PostCSS, autoprefixer) to scale styles across teams.
Prerequisites

Basic familiarity with a text editor and the web (browsers). No prior programming experience required, although some exposure to JavaScript helps when integrating with dynamic behavior.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of guided lessons and practical labs, plus optional mini-projects building a small site or component library.

Difficulty

Beginner → Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Write semantic HTML structures using headings, lists, forms and landmarks correctly.
  • Create responsive layouts using Flexbox and Grid and understand when to use each.
  • Build accessible UI components (buttons, forms, navigation) with proper ARIA and keyboard behavior.
  • Organize CSS for scale, apply preprocessors or PostCSS when useful, and automate cross-browser compatibility.
Detailed syllabus
  1. HTML fundamentals: document structure, semantic tags (header, nav, main, article, section, footer), inline vs block elements, forms and microdata.
  2. CSS basics: selectors, specificity, the box model, normal flow and positioning.
  3. Modern layouts: Flexbox patterns (centering, fluid rows, equal-height columns) and CSS Grid for two-dimensional layouts.
  4. Responsive design: media queries, mobile-first techniques, fluid typography, container queries (where available) and progressive enhancement.
  5. Styling architecture: naming conventions (BEM), modular CSS, CSS variables for themeability and approaches for component-scoped styles.
  6. Accessibility & semantics: semantic markup benefits, ARIA patterns, keyboard interaction, color contrast and screen reader testing.
  7. Tooling & performance: minification, critical CSS, code-splitting styles, autoprefixer, and build tool integration.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Build a small multi-section landing page using semantic HTML with header, hero, features and footer sections.
  2. Implement a responsive layout that reflows between mobile, tablet and desktop using Flexbox for the navigation and Grid for the feature gallery.
  3. Create a reusable card component styled via CSS custom properties and a simple naming convention (BEM or utility classes).
  4. Enhance accessibility: add skip links, aria-labels where needed, test keyboard navigation and validate color contrast.
  5. Integrate a basic build step (PostCSS/autoprefixer) and measure performance improvements from minified CSS and critical CSS extraction.
Example
<header>
  <nav>
    <ul><li><a href="#">Home</a></li> ...</ul>
  </nav>
</header>

/* CSS: simple grid */
.features { display: grid; grid-template-columns: repeat(auto-fit, minmax(220px, 1fr)); gap: 1rem; }
Assessment

Deliver a small static site and a short writeup that includes: semantic HTML choices, responsive layout strategies, accessibility checks performed and a performance checklist. Include source files and a README describing how to build and preview the site locally.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Prefer semantic HTML over presentational tags — semantics helps accessibility and search engines.
  • Avoid over-nesting and excessive specificity in CSS; keep selectors flat to improve maintainability.
  • Design mobile-first; start with small screens and progressively enhance layout for larger viewports.
  • Test across real devices and with assistive tools; simulated environments may miss important usability issues.
Resources
  • MDN Web Docs for HTML/CSS, A11y Project resources, Flexbox and Grid guides, and tooling docs (PostCSS, autoprefixer, CSS custom properties).
web-js:
Overview

JavaScript is the dominant language of the web and the primary way to build interactive, dynamic user experiences in browsers. This module teaches core JavaScript fundamentals and practical patterns needed to build modern web applications: language basics (variables, functions, objects), asynchronous programming (callbacks, promises, async/await), DOM manipulation, event-driven programming, and modular code organization. The emphasis is on practical skills that lead to robust, testable code and a clear migration path into frameworks and modern tooling.

Objectives
  • Understand JavaScript core concepts: types, scope, closures, prototypes and the event loop.
  • Be able to write asynchronous code using Promises and async/await and manage common pitfalls like race conditions and error handling.
  • Manipulate the DOM safely and efficiently, and implement accessible interactive components.
  • Organize code with modules and adopt basic testing and debugging workflows.
Prerequisites

Comfort with HTML & CSS basics. No prior JavaScript experience required, but familiarity with programming fundamentals helps.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours of guided lessons, exercises and small projects, plus optional deeper dives into frameworks (React/Vue/Svelte).

Difficulty

Beginner → Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Write idiomatic JavaScript using ES6+ syntax (let/const, arrow functions, template literals, destructuring).
  • Implement asynchronous flows with Promises and async/await, and understand microtasks vs macrotasks in the event loop.
  • Build and test DOM-driven UI components and manage state in straightforward applications.
  • Use modules and bundlers to organize code for production, and apply basic testing with a unit-test framework.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Language fundamentals: primitives, objects, arrays, functions, scope and closures.
  2. Advanced concepts: prototype inheritance, classes, modules (ESM), and runtime tasks.
  3. Asynchronous programming: callbacks, Promises, async/await, race/timeout patterns and cancellation strategies.
  4. DOM & events: querying the DOM, event delegation, handling user input and updating the UI efficiently.
  5. Tooling & quality: linting, bundling, source maps, unit testing and debugging tools in the browser.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Create a small interactive widget (todo list or tabs) that manipulates DOM and persists data to localStorage.
  2. Refactor callbacks to Promises and then to async/await; add loading/error states and cancellation logic for long-running tasks.
  3. Organize code into modules and configure a simple bundler (Vite/Parcel/Webpack) for development and production builds.
  4. Add unit tests for core logic (e.g., adding/removing items) and use the browser DevTools to debug runtime issues.
Example
async function fetchData(url){
  try{
    const res = await fetch(url);
    if(!res.ok) throw new Error('network');
    return await res.json();
  } catch(e){ console.error(e); throw e; }
}
Assessment

Deliver a compact interactive app with source organized into modules, a build script, and unit tests. Provide a README describing architecture choices, how to run locally and testing instructions.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Manage async errors explicitly; uncaught promise rejections lead to silent failures.
  • Avoid manipulating the DOM excessively—batch updates or use requestAnimationFrame for smoother rendering.
  • Prefer small, testable functions to monolithic scripts—this improves maintainability and enables unit testing.
Resources
  • MDN JavaScript guide, ECMAScript proposals, browser DevTools tutorials, and introductory books like You Don’t Know JS (YDKJS) for deeper understanding.
web-backend:
Overview

Backend & APIs teach the server-side fundamentals that power web applications: building HTTP services, designing RESTful or GraphQL APIs, data persistence, authentication and deployment. This module focuses on practical backend engineering that supports front-end experiences and production requirements such as scalability, observability and security. You will learn to design clean APIs, implement them with a modern framework, persist data in relational or document stores, and deploy a service with basic CI/CD and monitoring.

Objectives
  • Understand HTTP fundamentals, request/response lifecycle and API design principles.
  • Build a simple REST or GraphQL API backed by a database and apply common patterns for CRUD, pagination and filtering.
  • Implement authentication and authorization (session-based, token-based, OAuth basics).
  • Deploy and operate a service with logging, metrics and a simple CI pipeline.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with programming (JavaScript, Python, or similar) and basic knowledge of web client behavior. Some SQL knowledge is helpful for data persistence topics.

Duration

Approximately 10–14 hours including labs that build, test and deploy a minimal API service.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Design API contracts with clear resources and error semantics and implement them using a web framework (Express, FastAPI, etc.).
  • Model data in a relational or document database and implement migrations and basic indexing strategies.
  • Secure endpoints using authentication, validate inputs and handle errors gracefully.
  • Instrument service health with logs and metrics and deploy to a container or cloud instance with a simple CI/CD flow.
Detailed syllabus
  1. HTTP & API design: methods, status codes, idempotency, resource modeling and pagination conventions.
  2. Server frameworks: routing, middleware, validation, and organizing handlers for testability.
  3. Persistence: relational vs document choices, ORMs/queries, migrations and connection pooling basics.
  4. Security: input validation, auth patterns (JWT, sessions), rate limiting and secure storage of secrets.
  5. Deployment & observability: containerization, environment management, logging, health checks and basic metrics.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a small REST API for a resource (e.g., tasks or profiles) with endpoints for create/read/update/delete.
  2. Persist data to a database and add migrations; include basic query parameters for filtering and pagination.
  3. Add authentication and role-based access checks for restricted endpoints.
  4. Containerize the app, write unit/integration tests, and create a simple CI job that runs tests and builds an image.
Example
# pseudocode (Express)
app.post('/items', validate, async (req,res)=>{
  const item = await db.insert('items', req.body);
  res.status(201).json(item);
});
Assessment

Deliver a small API with documentation (OpenAPI/Swagger or README), containerized image, tests and deployment instructions. Include a short operations checklist for monitoring and common failure modes.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Validate and sanitize inputs at the boundary; never trust client data.
  • Design idempotent endpoints for safe retries and automation.
  • Keep secrets out of source control and rotate them when possible; use environment variables or secret stores for runtime configuration.
Resources
  • HTTP RFCs, framework documentation (Express/FastAPI), OWASP guides for web security and platform docs for containerization and deployment.
web-ci:
Overview

CI/CD & Hosting covers the practices and tooling to continuously build, test and deploy web applications. The objective is to enable fast, safe, and repeatable delivery of code to staging and production. This module introduces continuous integration concepts, automated testing strategies, container builds, artifact registries, and simple deployment targets (static hosting, container platforms). You will learn how to design pipelines that run tests, build artifacts and deploy with safety mechanisms such as feature flags and canary rollouts.

Objectives
  • Understand CI fundamentals: automated builds, test runs, artifact generation and pipeline orchestration.
  • Design a pipeline that runs unit/integration tests, builds production artifacts and optionally deploys to staging.
  • Containerize applications and publish images to a registry, and deploy to a simple hosting target (static host, cloud container service or serverless).
  • Adopt deployment safety patterns (canaries, feature flags, blue/green) and monitoring to validate releases.
Prerequisites

Comfort with command-line tools, basic build tools and familiarity with a version control system (Git). Experience building an app or service to be deployed is needed for hands-on labs.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of lessons and practical pipelines, plus a deployment exercise to a chosen host.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Create a CI pipeline that runs tests and produces deployable artifacts automatically on merges.
  • Containerize an application with a minimal Dockerfile and push it to a container registry.
  • Implement a basic deployment to a staging environment and add health checks and rollback steps.
  • Use feature flags or simple routing strategies to reduce release risk and measure rollout health.
Detailed syllabus
  1. CI basics: YAML pipelines, runners/agents, secrets management in CI, and caching strategies for performance.
  2. Testing in CI: running unit tests, integration tests and end-to-end tests, and failing fast when quality gates are broken.
  3. Building artifacts: container images, static site builds and versioned artifacts in registries.
  4. Deployment targets: static hosting (CDN), container platforms, serverless functions and basic orchestration strategies.
  5. Release strategies: blue/green, canary, and feature flags to decouple deployment from release.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Create a pipeline configuration that runs tests on pull requests and builds artifacts on merge to main.
  2. Write a Dockerfile for the app, build and push the image to a registry from CI, and deploy to a staging target.
  3. Implement a simple health-check endpoint and a rollback pipeline step if health checks fail after deployment.
  4. Introduce a feature flag for a new UI and perform a controlled rollout with traffic percentage or user targeting.
Example
# pseudocode CI step
- name: Run tests
  run: npm test
- name: Build image
  run: docker build -t repo/app:$GITHUB_SHA .
- name: Push
  run: docker push repo/app:$GITHUB_SHA
Assessment

Deliver a repository with a CI pipeline file, Dockerfile, and deployment instructions. Provide a short runbook describing how to run the pipeline, how to promote to production and how to roll back failing releases.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Keep pipelines fast—slow pipelines discourage frequent runs. Use caching and selective tests to speed CI.
  • Store credentials securely and never print secrets in CI logs.
  • Automate rollbacks where possible and ensure runbooks are clear for human intervention when needed.
Resources
  • CI/CD provider docs (GitHub Actions/GitLab CI), Docker and container registry guides, and feature flagging tools and patterns.
web-pwa:
Overview

Progressive Web Apps (PWAs) combine web reach with app-like capabilities: offline support, installability, background sync and fast load performance. This module focuses on turning a modern web app into a PWA using service workers, manifest files and caching strategies while keeping accessibility and progressive enhancement in mind. You will learn how to design for unreliable networks, optimize for perceived performance and implement best practices to make your web app feel native across devices.

Objectives
  • Understand PWA fundamentals: service workers, web app manifest and installability heuristics.
  • Implement offline-first strategies and caching policies that balance freshness and resilience.
  • Add installability and native-like UX affordances while preserving accessibility and progressive enhancement.
  • Measure PWA quality via performance metrics (LCP, FID, TBT) and real-world offline tests.
Prerequisites

Good familiarity with HTML, CSS and JavaScript and basic experience deploying web apps. Knowledge of service workers is helpful but not required.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours including labs to add PWA features to an existing web app and measure real-world improvements.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Add a web app manifest and service worker to enable offline caching and installability.
  • Design caching strategies (cache-first, network-first, stale-while-revalidate) appropriate to resource types.
  • Optimize runtime performance and boot time to improve user experience on mobile devices.
  • Test PWA behaviors in offline and poor-network scenarios and use Lighthouse to validate improvements.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Service worker basics: lifecycle, scope, fetch handling, message passing and update strategies.
  2. Caching strategies: static asset caching, runtime caching for API responses, background sync and stale-while-revalidate patterns.
  3. Installability: web manifest details, shortcuts, display modes and prompt flows for UX-friendly installation.
  4. Performance and measurement: Lighthouse audits, Core Web Vitals and strategies to reduce TTFB and improve LCP/FID.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Add a web app manifest with icons, name and start_url so the app can be installed.
  2. Register a service worker and implement a cache-first strategy for static assets and a network-first strategy for API data with fallback to cached results.
  3. Test offline behavior, implement a graceful offline UI, and add a background sync task for queued user actions.
  4. Run Lighthouse and iterate on improvements that target performance, accessibility and best practices for PWAs.
Example
// service worker skeleton
self.addEventListener('install', evt => { evt.waitUntil(caches.open('v1').then(c => c.addAll(['/','/styles.css','/app.js']))); });
self.addEventListener('fetch', evt => { evt.respondWith(caches.match(evt.request).then(r => r || fetch(evt.request))); });
Assessment

Deliver an enhanced web app with a working manifest and service worker, evidence of offline behavior, Lighthouse scores before and after changes and a short report outlining caching choices and fallback behaviors.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Be cautious with aggressive caching—stale data can confuse users; implement clear update strategies.
  • Handle service worker updates carefully; use versioning or skipWaiting/clients.claim patterns with care.
  • Test on real devices and network throttling; emulators miss certain platform quirks.
Resources
  • MDN service worker docs, Google Lighthouse guides, and PWA case studies demonstrating offline strategies and installability UX patterns.
web-ts:
Overview

TypeScript adds static types to JavaScript, improving development ergonomics, tooling and large-scale maintainability. This module introduces TypeScript fundamentals and practical adoption strategies for existing codebases. You will learn type basics, advanced type constructs, how to integrate TypeScript with bundlers and frameworks, and how to migrate incrementally. Emphasis is on pragmatic typing that catches common errors early while keeping developer productivity high.

Objectives
  • Learn TypeScript syntax, type annotations, interfaces, and type inference to write safer code.
  • Use advanced types where appropriate (union, intersection, mapped, conditional types) and adopt patterns for typed APIs and Redux/store-like state.
  • Integrate TypeScript into build tooling and configure tsconfig for incremental adoption.
  • Migrate JavaScript projects incrementally and use tooling (type-checking, linters) to maintain type quality.
Prerequisites

Comfort with JavaScript and basic ES6+ features. Familiarity with a build tool (Vite/Webpack) helps for integration labs.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours of lessons and migration exercises, plus optional deeper work on advanced typing patterns.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Annotate functions and data structures with types and use interfaces to describe object shapes.
  • Adopt TypeScript in a project incrementally and configure tooling to support type-checking and editor integrations.
  • Use generics to write reusable, typed utilities and understand trade-offs of strict vs loose configurations.
  • Resolve common type errors and apply patterns for typed third-party integrations.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Type basics: annotations, primitives, arrays, tuples, enums and interfaces.
  2. Functions and generics: typed parameters/returns, overloads and generic constraints.
  3. Advanced typing: union/intersection, mapped types, conditional types and utility types (Partial, Readonly, Record).
  4. Tooling and migration: tsconfig options, allowJs, checkJs, declaration files and integrating with bundlers and frameworks.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Create a small TypeScript project or convert a simple JavaScript module and add type annotations progressively.
  2. Introduce generics for reusable data-processing utilities and add unit tests with typed assertions.
  3. Configure build tooling to emit type declarations and set up a strictness profile that balances safety and developer velocity.
  4. Document migration decisions and create a checklist for future conversions in a larger codebase.
Example
function map(arr: T[], fn: (t:T)=>U): U[] { return arr.map(fn); }
interface User { id: number; name: string; }
Assessment

Deliver a small TypeScript-enabled repo or a migration patch with a short migration plan, configuration files, and explanation of key type decisions. Include how to run type checks and tests in CI.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Start with non-invasive typing (Loose) then tighten rules over time; radical strictness can slow adoption.
  • Use incremental compilation and editor integration to surface issues early for developers.
  • Prefer explicit interfaces for public API surfaces and allow implicit types in internal code where appropriate to speed iteration.
Resources
  • TypeScript handbook, migration guides, and community patterns for typing common JavaScript libraries and frameworks.
web-performance:
Overview

Performance & SEO teach you how to make web pages fast, responsive and discoverable. This module covers front-end performance techniques, web vitals, critical rendering path optimization, asset optimization, caching strategies, and fundamentals of search engine optimization (SEO). The aim is to help you design fast-loading, resilient web experiences that also rank well and convert users. Practical exercises show how to measure performance, diagnose bottlenecks and apply targeted fixes that yield measurable improvements.

Objectives
  • Measure and interpret key performance metrics (LCP, FID, CLS, TTFB) using Lighthouse and real-user monitoring.
  • Optimize critical rendering path: reduce render-blocking resources, inline critical CSS and defer non-essential scripts.
  • Apply asset optimization: image formats, responsive images, compression, lazy loading and caching headers.
  • Understand basic SEO best practices: semantic HTML, structured data, meta tags and server-side rendering considerations.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with HTML/CSS and basic JavaScript. Access to a development site to run measurement tools is required for practical labs.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of guided material and optimization labs, plus follow-up monitoring of improvements in production.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Use Lighthouse and DevTools to identify bottlenecks and prioritize fixes based on impact.
  • Implement lazy loading for offscreen assets, use modern image formats (WebP/AVIF) and apply responsive image techniques.
  • Set caching headers and use CDNs effectively to reduce latency and bandwidth for repeat visitors.
  • Apply SEO basics to improve discoverability: title/meta tags, structured data and server-rendered content where appropriate.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Performance measurement: Lighthouse, WebPageTest, Chrome DevTools and real-user monitoring concepts.
  2. Critical rendering path: CSSOM/Render tree, blocking resources, and tools to extract critical CSS.
  3. Asset optimization: bundling, tree-shaking, HTTP/2 considerations, image optimization and font loading strategies.
  4. SEO fundamentals: crawlability, indexing, semantic markup, structured data (JSON-LD) and sitemap generation.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Run a Lighthouse audit on a sample site and record baseline metrics. Identify top three opportunities for improvement.
  2. Implement changes: defer non-critical scripts, inline critical CSS for the above-the-fold content, and lazy-load images.
  3. Optimize images and fonts, configure caching headers and deploy changes to a staging environment.
  4. Re-run audits and compare metrics; prepare a short report showing before/after metrics and estimated user impact.
Example
/* example: lazy-load image */
<img src="small.jpg" data-src="large.jpg" class="lazy" loading="lazy" />
Assessment

Deliver an optimization report with Lighthouse before/after, a list of applied fixes, code samples and an estimate of user-perceived improvements. Include deployment notes and monitoring recommendations.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Measure before you optimize—optimizations should be prioritized by impact, not by novelty.
  • Beware of premature image conversions or font strategies that harm compatibility; test across target browsers.
  • Use CDNs and caching headers to reduce global latency but monitor cache invalidation strategies carefully to avoid stale content.
Resources
  • Google Web Fundamentals, Lighthouse docs, WebPageTest guides and SEO best-practices from major search engines and SEO communities.
app-architecture:
Overview

App architecture is the set of high-level design choices that determine how a mobile or desktop application is structured, how its components interact, and how it will evolve over time. Good architecture balances separation of concerns, testability, performance, scalability and developer productivity. This topic provides a practical, engineering-focused roadmap for designers and developers to choose and implement architectures that fit the application’s complexity, team size and deployment constraints. The emphasis is on patterns and trade-offs—monolith vs modular apps, MVC/MVVM/MVI variants, layered vs clean/hexagonal architectures, and pragmatic use of components and services—so participants can translate requirements into clear structural decisions and an actionable implementation plan.

Objectives
  • Understand core architectural goals: modularity, maintainability, testability, performance and evolution.
  • Compare common architecture patterns (MVC, MVVM, MVI, Clean/Hexagonal) and know when to prefer each.
  • Design a component map and module boundaries that minimize coupling and enable parallel development.
  • Specify data flow, side-effect management and dependency injection strategies appropriate to the platform and team.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with the target platform (mobile or desktop), basic programming skills, and exposure to building small applications. Some experience with state management and unit testing is helpful.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours including readings and hands-on labs designing and implementing a small reference app.

Difficulty

Intermediate to advanced depending on depth.

Learning outcomes
  • Produce a high-level architectural diagram for a sample app showing modules, boundaries and data flow.
  • Implement a small app using a chosen architecture pattern and demonstrate separation of responsibilities.
  • Write unit and integration tests that validate business logic independent of UI concerns.
  • Document migration and scaling strategies for the architecture to support future features and team growth.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Principles: Single Responsibility, Separation of Concerns, Dependency Inversion, Composition over Inheritance and CAPEX vs OPEX trade-offs for maintainability.
  2. Patterns overview: MVC, MVP, MVVM, MVI and where they fit in mobile and desktop contexts. Pros/cons and common pitfalls of each.
  3. Clean & Hexagonal architectures: ports and adapters, how to isolate business rules, and strategies for testable boundaries.
  4. Module design: feature modules vs layer modules, public APIs for modules, avoiding cyclic dependencies and enforcing boundaries with linting and code reviews.
  5. Data flow and side effects: unidirectional data flow vs two-way binding, command/query separation, handling async side-effects and background work.
  6. Dependency management and DI: patterns for injecting dependencies, using DI containers/frameworks vs constructor/manual injection, lifecycle considerations.
  7. Performance & resource constraints: lazy loading modules, minimizing memory churn, cold starts and startup-time optimizations.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Design a simple task management app diagram: modules (UI, domain, data, network), key interfaces and data types.
  2. Choose an architecture (e.g., MVVM with Clean Layers) and scaffold a minimal app showing module boundaries and interfaces.
  3. Implement domain use-cases as pure functions/classes and write unit tests without UI dependencies.
  4. Hook up the UI layer with a thin adapter to the domain and add integration tests that exercise a happy path.
  5. Measure app startup time and refactor module initialization to reduce overhead (defer heavy work, lazy load resources).
Example
// pseudocode: Domain-layer use-case
class AddTaskUseCase {
  constructor(taskRepo) { this.taskRepo = taskRepo }
  async execute(input) {
    // validation and business rules
    const task = Task.create(input)
    return await this.taskRepo.save(task)
  }
}
Assessment

Deliver a small repository with a documented architecture diagram, the implemented app scaffold, domain tests, and a short migration plan describing how the system would evolve to support multiple teams and offline-first capabilities.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Start with pragmatic architecture: over-engineering early creates friction. Evolve modularity as the codebase and team grow.
  • Separate business rules from framework code—this yields easier tests and reduces lock-in.
  • Use interfaces/contracts for module boundaries and enforce them in code reviews and CI checks.
  • Document architectural decisions and rationale—this is invaluable for onboarding and future refactors.
Resources
  • Recommended readings: Clean Architecture, Patterns of Enterprise Application Architecture, platform-specific architecture docs and example repos demonstrating feature modules and clean boundaries.
app-native:
Overview

Native platforms cover the platform-specific skills required to build performant and idiomatic applications for iOS and Android (and optionally desktop native platforms). Building native apps requires an understanding of platform SDKs, lifecycle management, native UI paradigms, platform-specific tooling, and distribution pipelines. This topic focuses on practical techniques to design, implement and optimize native apps using official toolchains (Xcode/Swift, Android Studio/Kotlin) and platform-conforming APIs. The course balances core platform APIs (views, layouts, networking, persistence) with best practices for memory management, battery efficiency, accessibility and integration with device capabilities (sensors, cameras, push notifications).

Objectives
  • Understand the native app lifecycle and platform UI paradigms (view controllers/activities/fragments/composables).
  • Use platform tooling to build, debug and profile apps for performance and memory usage.
  • Implement persistent storage strategies (SQLite/Room, Core Data), network handling (HTTP, background sync) and secure storage for secrets.
  • Integrate device capabilities: location, camera, notifications and sensors, respecting privacy and permission flows.
Prerequisites

Comfort with programming in a modern language (Swift/Kotlin/Java) and familiarity with object-oriented/functional constructs. Basic understanding of HTTP and data serialization is helpful.

Duration

Approximately 10–16 hours including hands-on labs building and profiling a native app.

Difficulty

Intermediate → Advanced

Learning outcomes
  • Ship a minimal native app that follows platform UI/UX conventions and handles lifecycle events correctly.
  • Implement efficient data persistence and background sync with reasonable battery and network usage.
  • Write platform-specific tests and use profilers to identify CPU, memory and battery hotspots.
  • Follow platform guidelines for accessibility, permissions and privacy policies.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Platform fundamentals: app lifecycle, view hierarchies, event propagation, threading models and main loop constraints.
  2. UI patterns: native navigation, lists, gestures, animations and responsive layouts across device sizes and orientations.
  3. Persistence: local databases (Room/Core Data), simple caching layers and handling migrations.
  4. Networking & background: HTTP clients, retry/backoff policies, background fetch, WorkManager/BackgroundTasks and push notifications.
  5. Security & privacy: secure storage, encryption for sensitive data, handling permissions and GDPR-style notices.
  6. Testing & tooling: unit/UI tests, emulators/simulators, profiling CPU/memory/network and crash reporting integrations.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Build a simple notes or tasks app with native UI using RecyclerView/List and a simple details screen.
  2. Persist notes locally using Room/Core Data or SQLite and implement migrations for schema changes.
  3. Add synchronization to a mock backend with background sync and network resilience.
  4. Profile the app, find memory leaks, improve startup time and add accessibility labels and keyboard navigation where relevant.
Example
// Kotlin: simple coroutine network call
suspend fun fetchItems(api: Api): List {
  return withContext(Dispatchers.IO) { api.getItems().map { it.toDomain() } }
}
Assessment

Deliver a native app repo with installation instructions, test suite, performance profile screenshots and a short write-up describing how you handled lifecycle, persistence, background sync, and accessibility checks.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Respect main-thread constraints—UI work must be lightweight and offload I/O or heavy work to background threads.
  • Test on real devices—emulators can hide performance and sensor integration issues.
  • Carefully manage permissions and explain to users why capabilities are requested—this increases trust and reduces uninstall rates.
Resources
  • Official docs: Android Developers (Jetpack, Kotlin), Apple Developer (SwiftUI/UIKit, background tasks), platform profiling and testing guides.
app-cross:
Overview

Cross-platform app development enables teams to build applications that run on multiple platforms (iOS, Android, web or desktop) from a shared codebase. Frameworks such as React Native, Flutter, Xamarin and others offer distinct trade-offs in terms of performance, developer ergonomics, native integration and ecosystem maturity. This topic explores practical cross-platform approaches: choosing the right framework, structuring shared and platform-specific code, integrating native modules, and maintaining parity with platform UX expectations. The course provides pragmatic patterns to maximize code reuse while preserving platform-specific polish where it matters.

Objectives
  • Understand cross-platform trade-offs: runtime model (bridge vs compiled), performance, bundle size and native API access.
  • Structure codebases to share business logic, UI components and assets while keeping platform-specific adapters minimal.
  • Integrate native modules when platform capabilities require native performance or APIs.
  • Set up CI/CD for cross-platform builds and manage platform-specific dependencies and releases.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with JavaScript/TypeScript (for React Native), Dart (for Flutter) or C# (for Xamarin), and basic native platform understanding. Experience building small apps helps.

Duration

Approximately 8–14 hours including labs that build a cross-platform app and add native integrations.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Ship a small cross-platform app demonstrating shared UI and native module bridging.
  • Implement platform-specific styling and navigation patterns while keeping business logic centralized.
  • Configure multi-platform CI pipelines to produce platform artifacts and manage release signing.
  • Measure performance and know strategies to optimize hot paths with native code or optimized libraries.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Framework overview: React Native (bridge JS runtime), Flutter (compiled native), pros/cons and ecosystem maturity.
  2. Project structure: shared modules, feature directories, platform-specific implementations and build-time conditionals.
  3. Native integration: writing bridges or channels, packaging native libraries and fallback strategies when modules are unavailable.
  4. UI parity: handling platform conventions (navigation, gestures, typography) and testing for consistent behavior across devices.
  5. Build & release: code signing, provisioning profiles, app bundles/APKs, and automated pipeline considerations.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Create a cross-platform app scaffold (React Native or Flutter) with a simple list/detail flow and a shared domain layer.
  2. Implement a native plugin for a platform-specific capability (e.g., speech recognition or advanced camera control) and expose it to shared code.
  3. Set up CI to produce Android and iOS artifacts, add platform-specific tests and verify deployment steps.
  4. Profile the app and optimize rendering or bridge overhead, moving hotspot code into native or compiled modules as needed.
Example
// React Native: bridge example (JS side)
import { NativeModules } from 'react-native';
const { NativeSpeech } = NativeModules;
async function speak(text){ await NativeSpeech.speak(text); }
Assessment

Deliver a cross-platform repo with build scripts, documentation of native integrations, CI pipeline configuration, and a small performance report describing any native offloads you implemented and why.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Avoid trying to make a cross-platform app look identical across platforms—respect platform conventions to reduce maintenance and improve UX.
  • Use native modules sparingly—bridges add complexity; prefer pure-framework solutions when performance is acceptable.
  • Keep business logic platform-agnostic to maximize reusability and simplify testing.
Resources
  • Official framework docs (React Native, Flutter), community guides on bridging/native modules and CI/CD templates for multi-platform releases.
app-testing:
Overview

Testing for apps covers the strategies, tools and practices to ensure reliability, correctness and a good user experience across device variations and releases. Effective testing combines unit, integration, UI/e2e, and property-based tests with automated pipelines that run on CI. This topic emphasizes designing testable architecture, choosing appropriate test boundaries, using emulators and real device farms, and adopting test flakiness mitigation strategies. Participants will learn to write maintainable tests, integrate them into CI, and triage failures to keep confidence high for frequent releases.

Objectives
  • Understand different test levels: unit, integration, contract, UI/e2e and performance tests and appropriate use-cases for each.
  • Design apps for testability: dependency injection, mocking boundaries and small pure logic units.
  • Set up automated test runs in CI, including device/emulator matrix and strategies to reduce flakiness.
  • Use tooling to record and replay UI tests and capture diagnostics for failed runs.
Prerequisites

Comfort with the app’s programming language and basic testing concepts. Familiarity with a test framework (JUnit, XCTest, Jest, Flutter test) helpful.

Duration

Approximately 6–12 hours with practical exercises covering writing tests and integrating with CI device farms.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Write unit tests for business logic with near-100% coverage of key use-cases and integration tests for data flows.
  • Implement robust UI tests that verify important flows and run them reliably in CI using emulators or cloud device farms.
  • Design strategies for handling flaky tests including retries, isolation and better test design.
  • Create a testing pyramid and enforce quality gates in CI to prevent regressions.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Testing pyramid: unit tests at the base, integration tests in the middle, and E2E/UI tests at the top—costs and benefits.
  2. Unit testing techniques: mocking, fake implementations, dependency inversion and pure function design.
  3. Integration & contract testing: verifying interactions with databases, network layers and native modules using test doubles and local servers.
  4. UI/e2e testing: automated UI frameworks, idempotent selectors, test data management and device farms.
  5. CI integration: running tests in parallel, caching, handling secrets and gating merges on test results.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Write unit tests for core domain logic and run them locally with code coverage reports.
  2. Create integration tests that spin up a local test server (or use mocks) to verify end-to-end flows without UI.
  3. Author UI tests for a critical path using a UI testing framework; run them on an emulator and on a cloud device farm in CI.
  4. Analyze flaky test patterns, refactor tests and add stability measures (explicit waits, retry strategies, isolate shared state).
Example
// Jest example: unit test
test('adds two numbers', () => { expect(add(2,3)).toBe(5); });
Assessment

Deliver a repo with a suite of unit, integration and UI tests, CI pipeline configuration to run them, and a short report describing coverage, flaky tests and plans to maintain test quality as the project grows.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Favor fast unit tests—slow test suites discourage frequent runs and reduce feedback speed.
  • Make UI tests resilient: avoid brittle selectors and ensure deterministic test data setup/teardown.
  • Address flakiness by fixing underlying application issues where possible rather than masking with retries.
Resources
  • Testing docs for platform frameworks (XCTest, Espresso), Jest/Mockito for JS, Flutter testing docs and CI/device-farm provider guides.
app-state:
Overview

State management is central to building predictable, maintainable applications. The topic covers patterns and libraries to manage state in app development, including local component state, global application state, ephemeral UI state, and persistent caches. Whether using platform-native approaches (ViewModel/LiveData, StateFlow) or cross-platform solutions (Redux, MobX, Provider, Riverpod), the goal is to make data flow explicit, reduce bugs from implicit side-effects, and enable easier testing and reasoning about app behavior. This module explores multiple paradigms—unidirectional data flow, reactive streams, observable models—and practical trade-offs to choose a solution for a given app size and complexity.

Objectives
