  • Understand state categories: ephemeral UI state, session state, persisted state and server state.
  • Compare state management paradigms: unidirectional flow (Redux/MVI), observable/reactive models, and localized state hooks.
  • Design state models that are testable, serializable and compatible with offline-first approaches.
  • Implement caching and synchronization strategies between local and server state, handling conflicts and merges.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with app programming and basic asynchronous concepts (promises/coroutines/streams). Some knowledge of existing state libraries helps but is not required.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours, including labs implementing and testing state strategies in a small app.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Choose and implement an appropriate state management approach for a sample app and justify trade-offs.
  • Implement robust caching and reconciliation logic for offline usage and server sync.
  • Write tests that validate state transitions and edge-cases such as conflict resolution.
  • Document state invariants and data contracts for team consumption.
Detailed syllabus
  1. State taxonomy: UI vs domain vs persistence vs network state and where each belongs.
  2. Unidirectional data flow (Flux/Redux/MVI): actions, reducers, stores and view bindings and advantages for debugging and time-traveling state.
  3. Reactive & observable patterns: streams, backpressure, and usage in platforms that support reactive primitives.
  4. Offline & sync strategies: local-first design, conflict detection, last-write-wins vs CRDT approaches and merge policies.
  5. Testing state: reducers pure function testing, integration tests for sync flows and property-based testing for invariants.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement shared state for a todo app using a chosen pattern (Redux or reactive ViewModel) and centralize domain logic in reducers/use-cases.
  2. Add persistence for server-fetched items, implement offline adds/edits and a background sync job that reconciles local and remote changes.
  3. Write tests for reducers/use-cases and simulate conflict scenarios to validate merge strategy.
  4. Measure state update performance on large lists and apply pagination or diffing to reduce UI jank.
Example
// reducer example (pseudocode)
function todosReducer(state=[], action){
  switch(action.type){
    case 'ADD': return [...state, action.payload];
    case 'REMOVE': return state.filter(t => t.id !== action.payload.id);
  }
}
Assessment

Deliver a repo demonstrating your chosen state approach with tests, a short explanation of offline/sync strategy and a plan describing how the state model would scale as features and team size grow.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Avoid putting everything into global state—local component state is simpler and often preferable for ephemeral UI values.
  • Keep reducers/use-cases pure when possible to simplify testing and reasoning about behavior.
  • Document the state shape and invariants; this helps teams avoid accidental coupling.
Resources
  • Documentation for Redux, MobX, Riverpod, platform ViewModel/StateFlow guides and articles on offline-first sync strategies (CRDTs, OT).
app-distribution:
Overview

App distribution covers the end-to-end process of packaging, signing, publishing and updating applications to app stores and enterprise distribution channels. This topic addresses build pipelines, code signing, store metadata, release strategies, and post-release monitoring. It emphasizes reproducible builds, secure management of signing keys, staged rollouts and the importance of telemetry and crash reporting post-deployment. Practitioners will learn how to automate releases, manage versions, and design a rollout plan that balances speed with safety.

Objectives
  • Understand build artifacts and packaging formats (APKs/AABs, IPA, MSIX) and platform-specific signing requirements.
  • Design CI pipelines that produce reproducible, signed artifacts and automate store submissions when possible.
  • Plan release strategies: internal testing, beta tracks, staged rollouts and hotfixes.
  • Instrument releases with telemetry, crash reporting and feature-flag controls to manage risk post-launch.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with building apps, basic CI knowledge and access to platform developer accounts (Google Play, Apple Developer) for hands-on labs.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours including labs to configure CI/CD for releases and to perform a staged rollout.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Create a CI pipeline that builds, signs and archives release artifacts, and optionally uploads to test tracks.
  • Manage signing keys securely, rotate when needed and document procedures for emergency key revocation.
  • Implement staged rollouts and monitoring dashboards to detect regressions quickly and support fast rollbacks.
  • Prepare store metadata, screenshots and localized descriptions aligned with app store guidelines.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Artifact formats and signing: differences between APK/AAB and IPA, signing workflows and platform constraints.
  2. CI/CD for distribution: automating build, test, sign and publish steps and secrets handling best practices.
  3. Release tracks and strategies: alpha/beta/internal testing, staged rollouts, canaries and rollback mechanisms.
  4. Post-release: telemetry, crash reporting, user feedback loops and handling OS-level compatibility changes.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Configure CI to build a release artifact and sign it securely using CI secrets or a secure key store.
  2. Upload artifact to an internal testing track and verify installation on test devices via distribution links.
  3. Perform a staged rollout to a small percentage of users, monitor crash rates and key metrics, then expand or rollback based on observations.
  4. Create a release checklist and runbook describing steps to promote, monitor and roll back releases.
Example
# pseudocode: CI release steps
- run: tests
- run: build --release
- run: sign-artifact --key $SIGNING_KEY
- run: upload-to-store --track beta --api-token $STORE_TOKEN
Assessment

Deliver a CI configuration that produces signed artifacts, a documented release checklist, screenshots of successful uploads to an internal test track and a short report describing monitoring and rollback plans.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Protect signing keys—losing them or leaking them can be catastrophic. Use hardware-backed stores or secure key management services where possible.
  • Prefer staged rollouts to full releases; they significantly reduce blast-radius for regressions.
  • Automate as much as possible but keep human checks for store metadata and legal/privacy changes that may require review.
Resources
  • Platform docs for Google Play and Apple App Store Connect, CI/CD provider guides and best-practice articles on secure key management and staged rollouts.
app-uiux:
Overview

Native UI/UX focuses on designing and building user interfaces that feel native to the platform and provide excellent user experience. This topic blends design principles with engineering practices: understanding platform conventions, accessibility, motion and interaction design, responsive layouts, and efficient asset pipelines. Engineers will learn how to collaborate with designers, implement designs faithfully while preserving performance and accessibility, and create reusable component libraries that encapsulate interaction patterns and theming.

Objectives
  • Understand platform design systems and conventions (iOS Human Interface Guidelines, Material Design) and apply them to build intuitive UIs.
  • Create reusable UI components with consistent theming and accessibility support.
  • Implement motion and micro-interactions that enhance usability while keeping performance implications in mind.
  • Collaborate effectively with designers, translating Figma/Sketch artifacts into production-ready components and responsive layouts.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with native UI frameworks (SwiftUI/UIKit, Android Views/Compose) or cross-platform equivalents and basic design literacy.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours including practical labs implementing a small design system and components.

Difficulty

Intermediate

Learning outcomes
  • Build a small component library (buttons, inputs, lists, modals) with accessibility labels and documented states.
  • Implement responsive layouts and adaptive navigation that respect platform patterns.
  • Use motion to communicate state changes and affordances while avoiding excessive animation that harms performance.
  • Integrate design tokens and theming for light/dark modes, high-contrast and localization readiness.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Platform conventions: navigation patterns, gestures, typographic scales and platform-native affordances.
  2. Design tokens: color, spacing, radius, elevation and how to map them to platform styling systems.
  3. Accessibility: semantic roles, screen reader labels, dynamic type, color contrast and keyboard/assistive navigation.
  4. Motion design: easing, choreography, and performance-friendly animation techniques.
  5. Collaboration: design handoff, tokens, redlines and maintaining parity between design system and implementation.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a small design system with core components and export a token set (colors, spacing, typography).
  2. Create a responsive screen that adapts to different device sizes and orientation changes.
  3. Add accessibility annotations, test with screen readers and ensure color contrast compliance.
  4. Implement subtle motion for state transitions (e.g., list reordering) and measure frame rates to avoid jank.
Example
// pseudocode: accessible button component
<button aria-label="Save" disabled={isSaving}>{isSaving ? 'Saving…' : 'Save'}</button>
Assessment

Deliver a small component library with documentation, examples of screens using the components, accessibility test results and a short design-implementation parity report showing how tokens map to code.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Respect platform conventions—users expect familiar navigation, gestures and controls for each platform.
  • Test accessibility early and often; retrofitting accessibility later is costly and error-prone.
  • Keep animations purposeful—avoid gratuitous motion that distracts or degrades performance.
Resources
  • Platform design guidelines (Apple HIG, Material Design), accessibility checklists and resources for design-implementation workflows (Figma tokens, design-system docs).
ds-array:
Overview

Arrays and lists are the most fundamental data structures in computer science and software engineering. At their core, arrays provide a contiguous block of memory that stores elements of the same type and supports efficient indexed access, while lists—depending on implementation—offer flexible insertion and removal semantics. Mastering arrays and lists is essential because they underpin higher-level structures and algorithms: iteration patterns, partitioning, in-place transformation, two-pointer techniques, sliding windows, and many classic algorithmic solutions (sorting, searching, median-finding, subarray problems). This topic explores both the theory and practice of arrays/lists: memory layout, complexity considerations, common operations, typical pitfalls (off-by-one errors, bounds checks), and how to choose between alternatives when designing systems.

Objectives
  • Understand the essential properties of arrays and sequential lists, including memory layout, contiguous vs linked storage, and index-based access.
  • Analyze time and space complexity of core operations: access, search, insert, delete, and resizing.
  • Master common algorithmic patterns on arrays (two pointers, sliding window, prefix sums, partitioning) and build intuitive mental models for when to use each.
  • Learn practical engineering considerations for languages with manual memory management and languages with managed runtime (GC), including cache behavior and vectorized operations.
Prerequisites

Basic programming skills (loops, conditionals), familiarity with common language constructs (arrays, lists) and elementary algorithmic complexity (Big O). Some exposure to pointer/offset thinking helps for systems-level performance discussion, but is not required.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours of guided lessons and exercises, with optional deeper readings on memory/cache behavior and system optimizations.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement array-based data structures and dynamic arrays (resizing strategies like doubling) in a chosen language.
  • Solve classical array problems using efficient techniques (two-sum, subarray sums, windowed aggregates).
  • Measure and reason about performance trade-offs in practice, including cache locality and branch predictions that affect throughput.
  • Choose data representations suitable for production settings (immutable arrays for concurrency, memory-mapped arrays for large data, packed arrays for low-level I/O).
Detailed syllabus
  1. Array fundamentals: definition, indexing, bounds checking, zero/one-based indexing convention, contiguous memory model.
  2. Dynamic arrays: amortized resizing, doubling strategy, shrink policy and pitfalls (over-frequent shrinking).
  3. Operation complexities: O(1) access, O(n) insertion/deletion for arbitrary positions, O(1) append amortized for dynamic arrays.
  4. Patterns: two-pointer techniques for pair/search problems, sliding window for subarray aggregates, prefix-sum arrays for range queries, partitioning for quickselect/quick-sort.
  5. Memory & performance: cache locality, stride access patterns, vectorization opportunities and avoiding aliasing-induced slowdown.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a simple dynamic array from scratch (push, pop, get, set, resize) and run microbenchmarks to observe amortized append cost.
  2. Solve classical problems: two-sum with hash vs two-pointer on sorted arrays; maximum subarray sum (Kadane’s algorithm); longest substring without repeating characters using sliding window and frequency tables.
  3. Implement prefix sums and use them to answer range sum queries efficiently. Extend to compute range minimum/maximum using sparse table approaches for read-heavy workloads.
  4. Profile contiguous vs non-contiguous access patterns (sequential scan vs stride) and observe cache effects using timing tools or language profilers.
Example
// JavaScript pseudocode: sliding window for max sum window
function maxWindowSum(arr, k) {
  let sum = 0;
  for (let i = 0; i < k; i++) sum += arr[i];
  let max = sum;
  for (let i = k; i < arr.length; i++) {
    sum += arr[i] - arr[i - k];
    if (sum > max) max = sum;
  }
  return max;
}
Assessment

Deliver a short notebook or repository containing: a dynamic array implementation, solutions to 6–8 canonical array problems, benchmark results comparing strategies (e.g., hash vs two-pointer), and a short write-up analyzing performance trade-offs and memory behavior for your chosen language.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Beware off-by-one errors with index arithmetic; prefer clear loop invariants and use tests to assert boundaries.
  • Avoid repeated reallocations by choosing an appropriate growth factor (commonly 2) — small growth factors can cause frequent copies, large ones may waste memory.
  • When processing large arrays, prefer sequential access to maximize cache performance and reduce page faults.
  • When concurrency matters, immutable or copy-on-write arrays can reduce locking complexity at the expense of allocation pressure.
Resources
  • Classic references: CLRS for complexity foundations, language-specific docs (std::vector for C++, Array for JS), and practical articles on cache-aware programming.
ds-linked:
Overview

Linked lists are a fundamental linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes that contain a value and a reference (or pointer) to the next node (and in the case of doubly linked lists, to the previous node). Unlike arrays, linked lists allow constant-time insertion and deletion at arbitrary positions when a reference to the location is available, but at the cost of sequential access for indexing. Understanding linked lists is crucial for developers because they underlie many other structures (queues, stacks, adjacency lists) and expose low-level pointer manipulation concepts that inform safe handling of memory and references in higher-level systems.

Objectives
  • Understand singly and doubly linked lists, sentinel nodes, circular lists and the trade-offs between them.
  • Analyze operation complexities: O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions vs O(n) search for arbitrary index-based access.
  • Practice pointer/reference manipulation safely—avoiding leaks and dangling references, and using safe idioms in managed languages.
  • Learn common algorithmic patterns performed on lists: reversing, merging, cycle detection, partitioning and in-place reordering.
Prerequisites

Comfort with basic programming constructs and a willingness to reason about references/pointers. Familiarity with arrays and iteration will help contextualize differences.

Duration

Approximately 6–8 hours of lessons and exercises focusing on implementation and typical interview-style problems.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement singly and doubly linked lists with core operations and robust unit tests.
  • Solve standard linked-list problems: reverse list, detect and remove cycles (Floyd’s algorithm), find kth-from-end, merge sorted lists in O(n) time.
  • Understand memory safety considerations: how languages with GC simplify management and what to consider in manual memory environments.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Node structure and canonical operations: insertAfter, insertBefore, deleteNode, find, head/tail maintenance.
  2. Advanced manipulations: in-place reversal (iterative and recursive), pairwise swapping, partition lists around a pivot, and merging sorted lists.
  3. Cycle detection and removal: Floyd’s tortoise and hare algorithm and entry point detection.
  4. Use-cases and performance: when to pick lists vs arrays, impact on locality and iteration performance, and list-backed collections in standard libraries.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a singly linked list class with append, prepend, insert at position, remove by value and find operations. Add unit tests covering edge cases (empty list, single-node list, boundary indices).
  2. Implement list reversal and write tests verifying structure invariants. Compare recursive vs iterative approaches in terms of stack usage.
  3. Solve common interview problems: detect cycles, remove nth-from-end using two-pointer technique, merge two sorted lists without extra allocations.
  4. Profile a list-based queue vs an array-based queue for workloads that alternate many inserts and removes from the front.
Example
// pseudocode: reverse a singly linked list
function reverse(head) {
  let prev = null;
  let cur = head;
  while (cur != null) {
    let next = cur.next;
    cur.next = prev;
    prev = cur;
    cur = next;
  }
  return prev; // new head
}
Assessment

Deliver a repository with a linked-list implementation, a suite of unit tests and solutions to 6 standard linked-list problems. Include notes on when and why to prefer linked lists, performance caveats and memory considerations for your development language.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Avoid iterating to find previous nodes repeatedly—maintain tail pointers or use doubly linked lists when frequent backward traversal is needed.
  • Be cautious with pointer reassignment—test transitional states explicitly to avoid subtle corruption.
  • Remember that modern languages’ standard libraries often provide safe, optimized list implementations—use them unless a custom behavior is necessary.
Resources
  • Textbook chapters on linked lists, language-specific container guides, and interview problem collections that include linked-list patterns and solutions.
ds-tree:
Overview

Trees are hierarchical data structures composed of nodes connected by edges. Binary trees, binary search trees (BSTs), AVL/Red-Black balanced trees and more advanced variants (B-trees, segment trees, Fenwick trees) are pivotal across systems: they implement ordered maps/sets, indexes in databases, expression parsers, file-system hierarchies, and many algorithmic solutions. Mastering tree fundamentals provides powerful tools for representing hierarchical relationships, carrying out divide-and-conquer strategies, and supporting logarithmic-time inserts/lookups when balanced properly.

Objectives
  • Understand the structure and properties of tree types: binary trees, BSTs, balanced trees (AVL/Red-Black), B-trees for disk/memory hierarchy.
  • Analyze tree operations: traversal orders, insertion, deletion and rotation operations required for balancing.
  • Learn specialized trees for algorithmic tasks: segment trees and Fenwick trees for range queries, trie (prefix) trees for string indexing, and suffix trees/arrays for substring problems.
  • Explore practical applications: building indexes, evaluating expressions, implementing hierarchical UI, and representing recursive structures.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with recursion, arrays/lists and elementary algorithmic analysis. Basic knowledge of pointers or references helps when implementing node manipulations.

Duration

Approximately 8–12 hours, including implementation of BSTs and at least one balanced tree variant and one specialized tree like segment/Fenwick tree.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement BST insert/search/delete operations and reason about worst-case vs average-case behavior.
  • Implement a balanced tree (AVL or Red-Black) and demonstrate how rotations restore balance after updates.
  • Build a segment tree or Fenwick tree to support fast range queries and updates and compare trade-offs.
  • Apply trees to practical tasks: building an index for a small dataset or implementing an autocomplete engine with a trie.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Tree basics: nodes, height, depth, tree traversals (preorder, inorder, postorder, level-order) and recursion patterns.
  2. BST operations and degeneracy: when BSTs become linear and how balancing prevents worst-case degeneration.
  3. Balanced trees: rotation mechanics in AVL and color-based balancing in Red-Black trees—proof sketch of logarithmic height maintenance.
  4. Range query trees: segment trees for mutable ranges, Fenwick trees (BIT) for prefix sums and their update/query complexities.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a BST with insert, search, and delete. Add inorder traversal to retrieve sorted order and unit tests for edge cases.
  2. Implement an AVL tree or Red-Black tree variant and write tests that insert a sequence of values to force rebalancing; visualize tree heights before/after.
  3. Implement a segment tree to support range sum queries and point updates for an array of integers; benchmark against naive approaches.
  4. Build a simple trie for prefix-based lookup (autocomplete) and measure insertion and query times for a dictionary-sized dataset.
Example
// pseudocode: Fenwick Tree (BIT) update and prefix sum
function update(bit, idx, val) {
  while (idx <= n) { bit[idx] += val; idx += idx & -idx; }
}
function prefixSum(bit, idx) {
  let s = 0; while (idx > 0) { s += bit[idx]; idx -= idx & -idx; } return s;
}
Assessment

Deliver a repository containing implementations for BST and one balanced tree, a segment tree or Fenwick tree implementation, example applications (e.g., autocomplete with trie), and a report comparing performance and complexity trade-offs for each structure.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Balanced tree implementations are intricate—start with correctness over micro-optimizations and rely on tests to validate invariants.
  • Choose specialized trees (segment, Fenwick) when operations and constraints match: Fenwick for prefix sums, segment trees for general associative operations over intervals.
  • Consider memory vs speed trade-offs: pointer-rich trees have overhead—array-based implicit trees (heap-like) can be more cache-friendly for some workloads.
Resources
  • Standard algorithms texts (CLRS), open-source libraries for balanced trees, and interactive visualizers for tree rotations and segment operations.
ds-hash:
Overview

Hash tables (maps/dictionaries) are a cornerstone of practical programming and algorithm design. They provide expected O(1) time complexity for insertion, deletion and lookup operations under reasonable assumptions and are widely used for counting, indexing, memoization and as building blocks for higher-level structures. Understanding hash tables deeply—hash functions, collision resolution strategies (chaining vs open addressing), resizing policies, and load factors—is crucial to design performant systems and to avoid subtle correctness and security issues (e.g., hash collision DoS).

Objectives
  • Explain hash table mechanics: hashing, compression, collision handling and resizing strategies.
  • Implement basic hash table variants (separate chaining and linear probing) and analyze their performance characteristics.
  • Understand practical considerations: choosing/implementing hash functions, avoiding pathological collisions, and memory usage trade-offs.
  • Apply hash tables to real problems: frequency counting, memoization, and efficient set operations on large datasets.
Prerequisites

Comfort with arrays and basic algorithmic complexity. Some exposure to low-level bit operations is helpful for understanding hash mixing and bitwise compression functions.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours including implementations and experiments with load factor tuning and performance testing.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement a hash table with chaining and one with open addressing. Demonstrate resizing strategies and compute empirical load factor effects.
  • Select or design appropriate hash functions for composite keys and understand language-provided hash utilities.
  • Use hash tables effectively for algorithmic tasks like two-sum, counting distinct values, and caching/memoization in dynamic programming.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Hash function basics: deterministic mapping, uniformity, mixing and domain-specific considerations (strings, tuples, large ints).
  2. Collision resolution: chaining (linked lists/buckets) vs open addressing (linear probing, quadratic probing, double hashing) and their cache behavior.
  3. Resizing & load factors: rehashing strategies, amortized cost of resizing, and practical thresholds for performance.
  4. Security and robustness: protecting against hash collision attacks, randomized hashing (salted hashes) and alternatives for adversarial inputs.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a simple hash table using separate chaining, supporting put/get/delete operations and automatic resizing.
  2. Implement an open-addressing variant and compare probe sequences, clustering effects, and cache locality with chaining implementation.
  3. Benchmark both implementations under uniform and adversarial workloads; observe how load factor and hash quality affect throughput.
  4. Use a hash table in algorithmic problems: anagram grouping, two-sum, and sliding-window frequency tracking; show how hash maps simplify implementations.
Example
// pseudocode: simple chaining hash map
function put(map, key, value) {
  let idx = hash(key) % map.capacity;
  let bucket = map.buckets[idx];
  for (node of bucket) if (node.key == key) { node.value = value; return; }
  bucket.push({key,value}); map.size++;
  if (map.size / map.capacity > loadFactor) resize(map);
}
Assessment

Deliver a repo with two hash table implementations, benchmark scripts demonstrating performance across workloads, solutions to algorithmic tasks that use hash maps, and a short write-up recommending practical default settings (load factor, initial capacity) for a chosen runtime environment.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • A poor hash function results in clustering and degraded performance—use well-tested hash functions from standard libraries unless you have special constraints.
  • Open addressing can be more cache-friendly but is sensitive to clustering; chaining has simpler deletion semantics and more robust behavior under high load.
  • Protect public-facing hash tables from adversarial collisions—apply randomized hashing or fallbacks when suspicious patterns are detected.
Resources
  • Language standard library docs for map/dict types, articles on hashing strategies, and papers on collision-resistant hashing and hash table implementations.
ds-heap:
Overview

Heaps and priority queues are abstract data types that allow efficient retrieval of the highest- or lowest-priority element. The binary heap is the canonical implementation: a nearly complete binary tree stored in an array, supporting O(log n) insertion and removal of the extreme element and O(1) access to the max/min. Heaps underpin many algorithms—heap sort, Dijkstra’s shortest path, event schedulers, streaming top-k computations—and are a key building block in both algorithmic and systems contexts.

Objectives
  • Understand heap properties and array-based representation, including parent/child index arithmetic and invariants required to maintain the heap property.
  • Implement heap operations: push, pop, peek, and heapify, and analyze their time complexity and amortized behavior.
  • Explore specialized heap variants (Fibonacci heap, pairing heap) that offer improved theoretical bounds for certain operations and discuss practical trade-offs.
  • Apply heaps to algorithmic problems: priority-driven graph algorithms, streaming top-k, median maintenance and scheduling tasks.
Prerequisites

Familiarity with arrays and trees and basic algorithmic complexity; understanding of recursion and iterative sift-up/sift-down operations is helpful.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours including implementation, algorithmic applications and benchmarks.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement a binary heap and show how to build (heapify) an array in O(n) time using bottom-up sift-down.
  • Use heaps to implement priority queues and solve Dijkstra’s algorithm and streaming top-k problems efficiently.
  • Understand when advanced heaps (e.g., Fibonacci heaps) are appropriate and why simpler binary heaps are often preferred in practice due to simplicity and constants.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Binary heap structure: array-index mapping, parent/child relations and heap invariants for min/max heaps.
  2. Operations: sift-up (bubble-up) for insertion, sift-down for extraction, decrease-key/increase-key complexities, and heapify in O(n) time.
  3. Applications: heap sort, Dijkstra (priority queue usage for relaxation), scheduling and streaming algorithms for top-k.
  4. Advanced heap types: Fibonacci heap theoretical benefits vs implementation complexity; pairing heaps and skew heaps as practical alternatives.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a binary heap (min-heap) with push, pop, peek and heapify. Add tests verifying heap property after each operation.
  2. Implement heap sort using the heap and compare performance to language-native sort on small and large datasets.
  3. Implement Dijkstra’s algorithm using your heap-based priority queue and benchmark against naive O(n^2) methods.
  4. Implement streaming top-k using a min-heap of size k and show its memory and time advantages for large data streams.
Example
// pseudocode: sift-down
function siftDown(heap, i, n) {
  while (true) {
    let left = 2*i + 1;
    let right = 2*i + 2;
    let smallest = i;
    if (left < n && heap[left] < heap[smallest]) smallest = left;
    if (right < n && heap[right] < heap[smallest]) smallest = right;
    if (smallest == i) break;
    swap(heap, i, smallest); i = smallest;
  }
}
Assessment

Deliver a repository with a binary heap implementation, tests, examples of algorithmic applications (heap sort, Dijkstra, top-k), and a short discussion comparing binary heap performance with other priority queue implementations in your chosen environment.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Remember that array-based heaps are cache-friendly and simple to implement—often the best practical choice.
  • For decrease-key-heavy workloads (e.g., certain graph algorithms), specialized heaps can reduce asymptotic cost but add implementation overhead.
  • Heap sort is O(n log n) and in-place but not stable; choose sorting accordingly based on stability needs.
Resources
  • Algorithm texts covering heaps, priority queues and graph algorithms, and practical libraries offering optimized priority queue implementations.
ds-trie:
Overview

Tries (prefix trees) are specialized tree structures for storing associative arrays where keys are usually strings. Each edge in a trie represents a character (or part) of the key, and nodes encode prefixes. Tries enable efficient prefix queries, auto-completion, longest-prefix matching and are used extensively in domains like routing tables, text processing, search engines and spell-checkers. They trade off memory (many nodes) for fast, deterministic query times that depend on key length rather than collection size.

Objectives
  • Understand trie structure, node layout, and operations: insert, search, delete and prefix enumeration.
  • Analyze memory/time trade-offs and implement compact variants: compressed tries (radix/patricia tries) and use of arrays vs hash maps for child storage.
  • Apply tries to practical problems: autocomplete, dictionary lookup, wildcard/pattern matching and longest-prefix routing.
  • Explore space-optimized representations for production use and trade-offs for speed vs memory (e.g., succinct data structures, DAWGs).
Prerequisites

Comfort with trees and recursion, and experience manipulating strings and maps in code.

Duration

Approximately 6–10 hours with implementations and experiments on memory optimizations for large dictionaries.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement a basic trie that supports insert, exact lookup and prefix enumeration.
  • Implement a compressed trie (radix tree) to reduce node count and improve memory efficiency for sparse alphabets.
  • Use tries to implement an autocomplete feature and measure latency and memory usage for realistic word lists.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Trie fundamentals: nodes, child maps/arrays, terminal markers and iteration strategies for prefix enumeration.
  2. Compressed tries: merging single-child chains into edge labels to reduce depth and node count; patricia trie basics.
  3. Memory optimization: using arrays for fixed alphabets, hash maps for dynamic alphabets, and pointer compression strategies.
  4. Advanced topics: deterministic acyclic word graphs (DAWG) for compact readonly dictionaries, succinct tries and external memory tries for very large datasets.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement a trie supporting insert and find operations for English words; add a method to list all words with a given prefix.
  2. Implement a radix/compressed trie and compare memory usage and lookup times with the naive trie on a large dictionary.
  3. Build an autocomplete demo that returns top-k matches by frequency (store counts at terminal nodes) and evaluate latency on common prefixes.
  4. Explore external-memory approaches: persist trie nodes to disk or use memory-mapped files for very large dictionaries and measure lookup costs.
Example
// pseudocode: insert into trie
function insert(root, word) {
  node = root;
  for (ch of word) {
    if (!node.children[ch]) node.children[ch] = newNode();
    node = node.children[ch];
  }
  node.isWord = true;
}
Assessment

Deliver a repository containing trie and compressed trie implementations, an autocomplete demo, benchmarks comparing memory and latency, and a short write-up recommending a trie variant for a given production scenario (e.g., in-memory autocomplete vs disk-backed lookup).

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Tries can be memory-intensive—use compressed variants or specialized encodings for production datasets.
  • For small alphabets (e.g., lowercase English), arrays for children yield the best performance but need space; use hash maps for sparse or large alphabets.
  • When throughput and memory are both critical, consider DAWGs or succinct representations for read-only dictionaries.
Resources
  • Textbook sections on trie and radix trees, open-source implementations for reference and articles on applying tries to search/autocomplete systems.
ds-unionfind:
Overview

The disjoint-set data structure—also known as union-find—is an efficient structure for tracking partitioning of a set into disjoint subsets. It supports two core operations: find (determine which subset a particular element belongs to) and union (merge two subsets). With path compression and union-by-rank (or size), union-find achieves nearly constant amortized time per operation (inverse Ackermann function), making it ideal for connectivity problems, Kruskal’s minimum spanning tree algorithm, network connectivity, image segmentation and many offline query problems.

Objectives
  • Implement union-find with path compression and union-by-rank/size and understand why these heuristics yield near-constant amortized complexity.
  • Apply union-find to algorithmic problems: connectivity queries, offline dynamic connectivity, Kruskal’s MST, and grouping/merging scenarios.
  • Analyze limitations: union-find solves connectivity but not dynamic deletions well, and discuss extensions or alternatives for different dynamic models.
  • Learn practical aspects: indexing elements, mapping objects to ids, and memory/layout choices to optimize performance.
Prerequisites

Comfort with arrays and recursion; basic graph algorithm knowledge is helpful for applications sections.

Duration

Approximately 4–8 hours with implementation and problem-solving exercises.

Learning outcomes
  • Implement a performant union-find with path compression and union-by-size/rank and add unit tests demonstrating amortized behavior.
  • Use union-find to implement Kruskal’s MST and solve connectivity problems efficiently.
  • Recognize when union-find is appropriate and when other dynamic connectivity data structures (link-cut trees, dynamic trees) are required.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Basic structure: parent array, rank/size array and representative/root concept.
  2. Optimizations: path compression (flattening the tree on find), union by rank/size to keep trees shallow and analysis of amortized time.
  3. Applications: Kruskal’s algorithm, offline connectivity queries via divide-and-conquer or sorting, and grouping problems like connected components in images.
  4. Limitations & variants: dynamic deletions, persistent union-find, and distributed variants for large-scale systems.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement union-find with path compression and union-by-size. Add tests that simulate many unions/finds and measure amortized performance.
  2. Implement Kruskal’s MST using your union-find and compare with Prim’s algorithm on sparse graphs.
  3. Solve an offline connectivity problem: given edge additions and queries asking whether two nodes are connected at certain times, process queries efficiently using union-find in an offline manner.
  4. Experiment with union-find on large random graphs and measure memory usage, find/union throughput and effect of heuristics.
Example
// pseudocode: union-find with path compression
function find(x) {
  if (parent[x] != x) parent[x] = find(parent[x]);
  return parent[x];
}
function union(a,b) {
  ra = find(a); rb = find(b);
  if (ra == rb) return;
  if (rank[ra] < rank[rb]) parent[ra] = rb; else if (rank[ra] > rank[rb]) parent[rb] = ra; else { parent[rb]=ra; rank[ra]++; }
}
Assessment

Deliver a repository with union-find implementation, tests, an MST example using Kruskal, offline connectivity solutions and a short analysis of performance with and without heuristics. Include notes on mapping complex objects to integer ids and memory/layout decisions for optimization.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Path compression implementations must be tail-recursive or iterative in languages without guaranteed tail call optimization to avoid stack overflow on very deep trees.
  • Union-find does not natively support deletions—consider alternate data structures for problems requiring deletions or persistent states.
  • Map domain objects to integer ids efficiently (hashing or direct mapping) to keep union-find arrays compact and efficient.
Resources
  • Algorithm texts covering union-find, Kruskal’s algorithm, and practical discussions on applications in graph problems and image processing.
alg-sorting:
Overview

Sorting is one of the most studied problems in computer science because it sits at the heart of so many systems: databases, search, analytics, memory management and numerous algorithmic pipelines. This expanded treatment goes beyond the simple statement “sort the array” and discusses pragmatic engineering considerations, algorithmic theory, data distributions, stability, memory hierarchy effects, external/parallel sorting and how sorting choices propagate into broader system design. Understanding why one sort outperforms another in a real environment requires attention to constant factors, branch predictability, cache locality, and the distribution of keys and duplicates. This article is intentionally comprehensive: it interleaves algorithmic descriptions, proofs sketches, tuning techniques and practical C++ implementations that are production oriented.

Why sorting matters

Sorting is a gateway to more efficient algorithms — once data is sorted you can do binary search, grouping, merge joins, and range scans efficiently. Sorting also plays a major role in external-memory algorithms (external merge sort) and parallel algorithms (sample sort, parallel merges). For many real-world workloads, the top-level question is not which algorithm is “theoretically best” but which one yields the best trade-offs for the given data size, memory constraints and parallel resources.

Fundamental trade-offs
  • Time complexity vs. practicality: comparison sorts have Ω(n log n) lower bound in the comparison model, but radix/counting sorts avoid comparisons for integer keys and can be linear.
  • Stability: stable sorts preserve relative order of equal keys — important for multi-key sorts and certain database operations.
  • Memory: in-place sorts minimize extra memory but may have worse locality or complexity in practice; out-of-place sorts (merge) use O(n) additional memory but provide stability and predictable behavior.
  • Branch prediction & cache: algorithms with predictable control flow (e.g., mergesort) may outperform algorithms with unpredictable branching on modern CPUs in some cases.
Core algorithms and when to use them
  1. Quicksort — Typically fastest in-memory comparison sort. Use randomized pivot or median-of-three to avoid pathological inputs. Suitable for general-purpose in-memory sorting when average-case performance matters and recursion depth is controlled (tail recursion elimination).
  2. Mergesort — Stable, predictable and easy to parallelize. Good for external sorting (k-way merge) and scenarios where stability is required.
  3. Heapsort — In-place with O(1) extra memory and O(n log n) worst-case; useful when memory is tight and worst-case guarantees are important.
  4. Radix/Counting sort — Linear-time for fixed-width integer keys or keys that can be decomposed. Ideal for keys with small alphabet or when many duplicates exist.
  5. External sort — For datasets larger than memory, use run-generation (replacement selection), then multi-way merge; tune run size for IO bandwidth.
Practical engineering patterns
  • Switch to insertion sort for tiny partitions (e.g., n < 32) since constant factors favor insertion sort on tiny arrays.
  • Use hybrid approaches: introsort (std::sort) starts with quicksort and switches to heapsort on deep recursion to guarantee O(n log n) worst-case.
  • For nearly-sorted inputs, consider natural mergesort that detects runs and merges them; avoids O(n log n) when input is already sorted.
  • Parallel sorting: divide and conquer with thread pools, or use radix/sample sort for large multicore systems.
Correctness and stability proofs (sketch)

Comparison sorts use pairwise comparisons — decision tree model yields Ω(n log n) comparisons in the worst case to distinguish n! permutations. Radix sorts avoid this by exploiting key structure, so they are not bound by the comparison lower bound.

Comprehensive C++ examples

Below are concise, idiomatic C++ examples illustrating typical implementations and patterns used for production-quality code. Each snippet is minimal but shows core ideas you can extend. These examples are intentionally clear rather than hyper-optimized; for real production use, integrate profiling and low-level tuning.

Quicksort (randomized pivot + insertion switch)
// filepath: example_quicksort.cpp
#include <bits/stdc++.h>
using namespace std;

// insertion sort for small ranges
void insertion_sort(vector<int>& a, int l, int r) {
    for (int i = l+1; i <= r; ++i) {
        int key = a[i]; int j = i-1;
        while (j >= l && a[j] > key) { a[j+1] = a[j]; --j; }
        a[j+1] = key;
    }
}

int partition(vector<int>& a, int l, int r) {
    int pivot = a[l + rand() % (r-l+1)];
    int i = l-1, j = r+1;
    while (true) {
        do { ++i; } while (a[i] < pivot);
        do { --j; } while (a[j] > pivot);
        if (i >= j) return j;
        swap(a[i], a[j]);
    }
}

void quicksort(vector<int>& a, int l, int r) {
    if (l >= r) return;
    if (r - l < 32) { insertion_sort(a,l,r); return; }
    int p = partition(a,l,r);
    quicksort(a,l,p);
    quicksort(a,p+1,r);
}

int main(){
    ios::sync_with_stdio(false);
    cin.tie(nullptr);
    vector<int> a = {5,3,8,1,2,7,4,6};
    quicksort(a,0,(int)a.size()-1);
    for(auto v:a) cout<<v<<" "; cout<<"\n";
}
Mergesort (stable, bottom-up)
// filepath: example_mergesort.cpp
#include <vector> #include <iostream> using namespace std;

void merge(vector<int>& a, vector<int>& tmp, int l, int m, int r) {
    int i = l, j = m+1, k = l;
    while (i <= m && j <= r) tmp[k++] = (a[i] <= a[j] ? a[i++] : a[j++]);
    while (i <= m) tmp[k++] = a[i++];
    while (j <= r) tmp[k++] = a[j++];
    for (k = l; k <= r; ++k) a[k] = tmp[k];
}

void mergesort(vector<int>& a) {
    int n = (int)a.size(); vector<int> tmp(n);
    for (int width = 1; width < n; width *= 2)
        for (int i = 0; i < n; i += 2*width) {
            int l = i, m = min(i+width-1, n-1), r = min(i+2*width-1, n-1);
            if (m < r) merge(a,tmp,l,m,r);
        }
}

int main(){ vector<int> a = {3,1,4,1,5,9,2}; mergesort(a); for(int v:a) cout<<v<<" "; cout<<"\n"; }
Heapsort (in-place)
// filepath: example_heapsort.cpp
#include <vector> #include <iostream> using namespace std;

void heapify(vector<int>& a, int n, int i){
    int largest = i; int l = 2*i+1, r = 2*i+2;
    if (l < n && a[l] > a[largest]) largest = l;
    if (r < n && a[r] > a[largest]) largest = r;
    if (largest != i){ swap(a[i], a[largest]); heapify(a,n,largest); }
}

void heapsort(vector<int>& a){ int n=a.size();
    for (int i = n/2-1; i >= 0; --i) heapify(a,n,i);
    for (int i = n-1; i > 0; --i){ swap(a[0],a[i]); heapify(a,i,0); }
}

int main(){ vector<int> a={9,4,7,1,3}; heapsort(a); for(int v:a) cout<<v<<" "; cout<<"\n"; }
Radix sort (32-bit integers)
// filepath: example_radix.cpp
#include <vector> #include <iostream> #include <algorithm> using namespace std;

void radix_sort(vector<unsigned int>& a){
    const int B = 16; const unsigned int MASK = (1u<>shift)&MASK]++;
        for (int i=1;i<(1<>shift)&MASK]] = a[i];
        a.swap(tmp);
    }
}

int main(){ vector<unsigned int> a={3,1,4,1,5,9}; radix_sort(a); for(auto v:a) cout<<v<<" "; cout<<"\n"; }

These examples are starting points — for production-ready sorting you will want to integrate profiling, branch-misprediction analysis and potentially low-level SIMD/vectorized kernels for hot paths. Additionally, sorting for large-scale systems involves external merges, careful IO tuning and parallel algorithms. Use std::sort (introsort) as a robust default and tune only when profiling reveals hotspots.

Next steps

Study the interaction between sorting and system architecture: memory bandwidth, NUMA, storage latency for external sorts and the impact of compression. Benchmark with realistic data (key distributions and duplicates) and iterate: profile, identify cache misses and branch misses, then optimize pivot selection or use hybrid algorithms. Sorting is an excellent area to practice rigorous engineering: implement, measure, validate and repeat.
alg-search:
Overview

Searching is the task of locating elements that satisfy a given condition in data structures. The simplest searching task—linear search—scans elements sequentially, but efficient searching leverages structure: binary search on sorted arrays, index structures (B-trees) for databases, hash-based lookups for expected O(1) time, and specialized search algorithms for strings and sequences (KMP, Boyer–Moore). This module explores fundamental searching techniques, their correctness proofs and complexity, and practical systems-level considerations such as index maintenance, concurrency, and caching. Students will learn how to match the search algorithm to the data shape and update patterns, and how to implement robust searching primitives and indices that support range queries, prefix/suffix queries and approximate matches.

Objectives
  • Understand binary search invariants and implement safe, bug-free variants.
  • Learn index structures: balanced search trees and B-trees for persistent storage and their trade-offs for read/write workloads.
  • Study string-search algorithms: naive, KMP, Boyer–Moore, Rabin–Karp and suffix-based approaches for substring and pattern matching.
  • Design search strategies for approximate/nearest-neighbor queries using specialized indexes (KD-trees, LSH).
Detailed syllabus
  1. Binary search: loop invariants, off-by-one traps, lower_bound/upper_bound semantics and how to generalize binary search to monotone predicates.
  2. Ordered indexes: BSTs, AVL/Red-Black trees, B-trees for disk-backed indexes, and trade-offs between fanout, node size and IO efficiency.
  3. Hash-based lookup: hash table mechanics, collision handling and when hash maps outperform ordered structures.
  4. String searching: prefix-functions in KMP, skip heuristics in Boyer–Moore, rolling-hash ideas in Rabin–Karp and suffix arrays/trees for full-text indexing.
  5. Nearest neighbor and similarity search: KD-tree basics, approximate techniques like locality-sensitive hashing, and use-cases for high-dimensional data.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement binary search (lower/upper bound variants) rigorously with tests for edge cases and monotone predicates.
  2. Build a small B-tree-like disk-backed index for sorted records and implement range queries and merges; simulate page reads to analyze IO behavior.
  3. Implement KMP and Boyer–Moore string search and compare throughput on large text corpora; analyze failure function construction costs.
  4. Prototype a KD-tree for 2D nearest-neighbor queries and compare exact vs approximate query time on synthetic datasets.
Example
// binary search lower_bound
function lowerBound(a, target) {
  let lo = 0, hi = a.length; // hi is exclusive
  while (lo < hi) {
    let mid = Math.floor((lo+hi)/2);
    if (a[mid] < target) lo = mid+1; else hi = mid;
  }
  return lo;
}
Assessment

Deliver implementations for lower_bound/upper_bound, a small B-tree index with range-query support, string-search algorithms with benchmarks on representative corpora and a KD-tree prototype for nearest-neighbor queries. Provide a write-up that explains why specific structures were chosen for given query/update workloads.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Binary search bugs are common—prefer canonical templates (lower/upper bound) and unit tests that exercise boundaries thoroughly.
  • B-trees and database indexes are tuned for page size and IO patterns—simulate realistic page sizes when evaluating external indexes.
  • String search performance depends heavily on alphabet size and text distribution—measure on representative corpora.
Resources
  • Algorithm texts, practical database index references, and libraries for full-text and nearest-neighbor search (Lucene, FAISS).
alg-graph:
Overview

Graph algorithms are a rich class of algorithms for reasoning about nodes and edges that model relationships in data. Graphs appear everywhere: social networks, transport networks, dependency graphs, and knowledge graphs. This module covers core graph algorithms—BFS/DFS, shortest paths (Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford), minimum spanning trees (Kruskal, Prim), connectivity, topological sorting, strongly connected components (Kosaraju/Tarjan) and matching. The course emphasizes both algorithmic complexity and practical engineering: efficient graph representations (adjacency lists vs matrices), memory and locality considerations for large graphs, and scalable approaches for very large graphs (external/streaming algorithms, parallel graph processing frameworks).

Objectives
  • Implement and reason about BFS/DFS traversals and use them to solve reachability, connected components and layering problems.
  • Implement shortest-path algorithms with appropriate data structures (priority queues) and understand when negative weights require different approaches.
  • Implement MST algorithms and understand cut/weight exchange arguments that justify correctness.
  • Learn SCC and topological sort algorithms applicable to DAG processing and cycle detection.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Graph representations: adjacency list/matrix, edge list and hybrid compressed formats for sparse graphs (CSR/CSC).
  2. Traversal fundamentals: BFS for unweighted shortest paths, DFS for discovery/finish times and back-edge detection.
  3. Shortest paths: Dijkstra with binary/heap optimizations, Bellman-Ford for negative edges, and A* for heuristically guided search.
  4. Spanning trees and connectivity: Kruskal & Prim for MST, union-find for component maintenance, and algorithms for bridges/articulation points.
  5. Advanced topics: max-flow/min-cut basics, matching algorithms (Hungarian, Hopcroft-Karp), and scalable graph processing patterns.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement BFS and DFS and use them to compute connected components, topological order, cycle detection and distance layers.
  2. Implement Dijkstra using a binary heap and then a Fibonacci-style heap or pairing heap (or use library) and compare performance on sparse vs dense graphs.
  3. Implement Kruskal’s MST using union-find and compare with Prim’s algorithm for dense graphs; test on random geometric graphs and road-network-like graphs.
  4. Apply SCC algorithms to dependency graphs to detect cyclical package dependencies and produce condensation DAGs.
Example
// pseudocode: BFS
function bfs(start) {
  let q = [start]; dist[start]=0; visited[start]=true;
  while (q.length) {
    let v = q.shift();
    for (w of adj[v]) if (!visited[w]) { visited[w]=true; dist[w]=dist[v]+1; q.push(w); }
  }
}
Assessment

Deliver a repo with implementations of BFS/DFS, Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, Kruskal/Prim and SCC algorithms, performance comparisons on graph families (random, scale-free, grid-like), and application examples (routing, dependency analysis). Include notes on memory layout and scaling strategies for large graphs.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Pick graph representations to match density—adjacency lists for sparse graphs, matrices for dense problems where constant-time edge checks matter.
  • Edge weights and negative cycles change feasible algorithms—use Bellman-Ford or detect and handle negative cycles explicitly.
  • Large-scale graphs require streaming, out-of-core or distributed frameworks—design for locality and minimize random IO.
Resources
  • Standard algorithm texts, graph libraries (NetworkX, igraph, SNAP), and parallel graph systems (Pregel, GraphX) for scaling experiments.
alg-dp:
Overview

Dynamic Programming (DP) is a technique for solving problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems and storing intermediate results to avoid exponential recomputation. DP transforms naive recursive solutions into efficient table-driven or memoized implementations and is central to optimization problems, sequence alignment, parsing and resource allocation. This module teaches the art of identifying DP structure (optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems), choosing state representations, deriving transitions, and optimizing for time/space. It also explores advanced DP patterns—divide and conquer DP, knuth optimization, monotone queues—and practical considerations for implementing DP robustly in production code.

Objectives
  • Recognize when a problem admits a DP solution and design appropriate state representations and transition recurrences.
  • Implement memoized recursion and bottom-up table-based DP and understand when each is preferable.
  • Apply optimization techniques such as space compression, monotone queue optimizations and convex hull tricks for certain cost structures.
  • Use DP in classic settings: knapsack, coin change, edit distance, longest increasing subsequence and interval DP for partitioning problems.
Detailed syllabus
  1. DP fundamentals: subproblems, recurrence relations, base cases and proof of correctness via induction.
  2. Implementation patterns: top-down memoization vs bottom-up tabulation; iterative refinement to reduce stack depth and memory.
  3. Space/time optimizations: using rolling arrays, compressing dimensions, and early termination heuristics.
  4. Advanced optimizations: divide-and-conquer DP optimization, monotone queue and convex hull trick and when they apply.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Solve classic DP problems: coin change (counting), 0/1 knapsack, longest common subsequence and edit distance with both memoization and tabulation.
  2. Implement LIS in O(n log n) and compare to O(n^2) DP; explain when patience sorting technique maps to DP states.
  3. Implement an interval DP example (matrix chain multiplication or optimal BST) to illustrate O(n^3) naïve costs and potential optimizations.
  4. Experiment with space compression and measure memory/time trade-offs on larger inputs.
Example
// pseudocode: edit distance (bottom-up)
for i in 0..n: dp[i][0]=i
for j in 0..m: dp[0][j]=j
for i in 1..n: for j in 1..m:
  if a[i]==b[j] dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]
  else dp[i][j]=1+min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1])
Assessment

Deliver solutions to a range of DP tasks (knapsack, LCS, edit distance, interval DP), a description of state design decisions and any applied optimizations, and benchmarks comparing memoized vs tabulated implementations. Include tests for edge cases and a short explanation of when DP is appropriate versus greedy or other approaches.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Define clear state variables—ambiguous state space often leads to incorrect recurrences or exponential blow-up.
  • Memoization is quick to prototype but watch recursion depth and memory; tabulation can be more memory efficient when ordering is known.
  • Consider greedy alternatives first—if greedy correctness can be proven, it is usually simpler and lower-overhead than DP.
Resources
  • Algorithm texts, competitive programming archives (for diverse DP patterns), and articles on advanced DP optimizations (Knuth, divide-and-conquer, convex hull trick).
alg-greedy:
Overview

Greedy algorithms construct solutions incrementally, choosing at each step the locally optimal choice with the hope it yields a globally optimal solution. When a greedy choice property and optimal substructure hold, greedy algorithms are simple, fast and often optimal, making them attractive for many practical problems. This module explains how to identify greedy-solvable problems, common greedy templates (sort-and-choose, earliest-deadline-first, interval scheduling, Huffman coding, activity selection), and rigorous methods for proving greedy correctness (exchange arguments, matroid theory). Additionally, we contrast greedy with DP and other approaches to clarify when greedy heuristics are insufficient and how to design provably optimal greedy solutions when possible.

Objectives
  • Recognize canonical greedy problems and choose the appropriate greedy strategy.
  • Use exchange and inductive arguments to prove correctness of greedy algorithms.
  • Implement classic greedy algorithms: interval scheduling, Kruskal’s MST, Huffman coding and activity selection.
  • Understand matroids as a unifying framework explaining why greedy works for certain classes of problems.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Greedy patterns: sort by key (value/weight ratio, deadlines, earliest finish) and make local choices; greedy by choice of data structure (priority queue for online greedy).
  2. Proving correctness: exchange argument technique, cut/cycle properties for MST, and counterexamples where greedy fails.
  3. Applications: Huffman coding for prefix-free compression, interval scheduling for maximal non-overlapping sets, coin change for canonical currencies vs counterexamples.
  4. Matroid theory: basis exchange property and greedy optimality for set systems that are matroids.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement interval scheduling (sort by finish time) and verify optimality on random intervals. Provide counterexamples if sorted by start time.
  2. Implement Huffman coding and compare compressed sizes for sample corpora versus naive fixed-length encoding.
  3. Implement Kruskal’s MST using a greedy edge-selection ordering and union-find to maintain components; test on weighted graphs.
  4. Explore coin change counterexamples: design coin systems where greedy fails and show dynamic programming solutions for optimal change.
Example
// interval scheduling sketch
sort intervals by end
let lastEnd = -inf
for interval in intervals:
  if interval.start >= lastEnd: choose interval; lastEnd = interval.end
Assessment

Deliver implementations for interval scheduling, Huffman coding and Kruskal’s MST, proofs/sketches of greedy correctness for each and a set of counterexamples where greedy fails (with explanations). Include performance tests and discussion about when to prefer greedy vs DP.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Greedy solutions should be accompanied by correctness arguments—don’t rely on intuition alone.
  • Test greedy heuristics on adversarial inputs; many heuristics fail silently on crafted cases.
  • Matroids provide a theoretical lens—if your problem maps to a matroid, greedy is likely optimal.
Resources
  • Algorithm textbooks, papers on matroids, and practical coding examples for greedy patterns and counterexamples.
alg-flows:
Overview

Flow algorithms compute how to route resources through networks subject to capacity constraints. Max-flow/min-cut is a central theme with applications in network design, bipartite matching, circulation problems and image segmentation. This module covers classical algorithms—Ford–Fulkerson (augmenting path), Edmonds–Karp (BFS augmentation), Dinic’s algorithm with level graphs and blocking flows, and push–relabel approaches. It also explores reductions: maximum matching as max-flow, min-cut duality, and practical optimizations for unit networks and bipartite matching (Hopcroft–Karp). Students learn to choose algorithms based on graph size, density and capacity distributions and to implement robust flow solvers with complexity and memory trade-offs in mind.

Objectives
  • Implement max-flow algorithms and understand theoretical time bounds and practical performance characteristics.
  • Use flow–cut duality to translate between max-flow and min-cut applications and reason about capacity bottlenecks.
  • Apply flow algorithms to matching problems, edge-disjoint path problems and circulation/reservations with lower/upper bounds.
  • Learn performance engineering: scaling to larger graphs with heuristics, layered networks and capacity-scaling improvements.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Flow basics: flow conservation, capacities, residual networks and augmenting paths; correctness and cut capacity relationship.
  2. Algorithms: Ford–Fulkerson conceptual base, Edmonds–Karp O(VE^2) improvement, Dinic’s O(V^2 E) worst-case but fast in practice with unit graphs, and push–relabel variants with gap heuristics.
  3. Matching & reductions: transform bipartite matching to flow, Hopcroft–Karp algorithm for O(E sqrt(V)), and min-cut extraction for partitioning tasks.
  4. Advanced topics: minimum-cost flow, successive shortest paths, potentials (Johnson’s reweighting), and applications in scheduling and resource allocation.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement Edmonds–Karp and Dinic’s algorithm; test on a variety of graphs including dense networks and unit-capacity random graphs.
  2. Solve maximum bipartite matching via flow reduction and compare with Hopcroft–Karp where appropriate.
  3. Implement a minimum-cost flow solver using successive shortest paths and potentials for small-scale assignment problems.
  4. Apply max-flow/min-cut to a concrete application (e.g., image segmentation toy or cut-based clustering) and visualize results.
Example
// Dinic-level graph BFS sketch
build level[] with BFS from source on residual edges with cap>0
then DFS to find blocking flows limited by level edges
repeat until no path
Assessment

Deliver implementations for Edmonds–Karp and Dinic, a bipartite-matching reduction and a minimum-cost flow solver example. Include benchmarks on classes of graphs, and a short report explaining choice of algorithm for each workload and space/time trade-offs.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Dinic is very effective on many practical instances but be mindful of memory for large residual graphs.
  • Careful implementation of residual edge arrays and reuse of adjacency vectors greatly improves speed and memory locality.
  • Minimum-cost flow can be numerically sensitive—use integer costs where possible and stable shortest-path routines.
Resources
  • Graph algorithm texts, references on Dinic/push–relabel, and libraries offering optimized flow implementations for production workloads.
alg-geometry:
Overview

Computational geometry deals with algorithms for geometric problems: point-in-polygon, convex hulls, polygon triangulation, nearest neighbors, Voronoi diagrams and line segment intersections. Geometric algorithms are crucial in graphics, simulations, GIS, robotics and spatial databases. This module introduces robust geometric primitives, numerical robustness issues, and canonical algorithms (Graham scan, Andrew monotone chain for convex hulls, plane sweep for intersections, Delaunay triangulation). Students learn to represent geometric objects, reason about precision, and apply algorithms to practical tasks such as collision detection, spatial indexing and map overlays.

Objectives
  • Master foundational geometric primitives: orientation tests, vector arithmetic and robust predicates for degeneracy handling.
  • Implement classic algorithms: convex hull, line-segment intersection via plane sweep, point location and nearest neighbor (KD-tree).
  • Understand numerical robustness and strategies to mitigate floating-point errors (integer coordinates, exact predicates, epsilon-tolerant comparisons).
  • Apply geometry algorithms to problems: polygon operations, spatial indexing and path planning basics.
Detailed syllabus
  1. Geometry primitives: orientation (cross product), distance metrics, axis-aligned bounding boxes and basic transforms.
  2. Convex hull algorithms: Graham scan and Andrew monotone chain with O(n log n) complexity; gift-wrapping and incremental hull methods.
  3. Plane sweep and segment intersection: event-based sweep line, balanced trees for the active set and handling degeneracies.
  4. Triangulation & meshes: ear clipping for polygons, Delaunay triangulation basics and Voronoi/Delaunay duality for nearest-neighbor structures.
Hands-on lab (step-by-step)
  1. Implement robust orientation and segment intersection primitives with comprehensive unit tests that exercise collinear and boundary cases.
  2. Implement monotone chain convex hull and visualize hulls for random and clustered point sets; measure runtime scaling and memory usage.
  3. Implement plane-sweep for reporting all segment intersections and apply to overlay tasks (map line overlay).
  4. Build a KD-tree for 2D nearest neighbor queries and benchmark against brute-force search on large point sets; discuss spatial indexing trade-offs.
Example
// orientation test
function orient(a,b,c) {
  return (b.x-a.x)*(c.y-a.y) - (b.y-a.y)*(c.x-a.x);
}
// positive -> ccw, negative -> cw, zero -> collinear
Assessment

Deliver implementations for orientation primitives, convex hull, plane sweep intersection and KD-tree nearest neighbor, visualization examples, and a short discussion on numerical robustness and strategies used to ensure correctness in degenerate cases. Include sample datasets and benchmark results.

Tips & Pitfalls
  • Floating-point robustness is the primary practical challenge—prefer integer arithmetic or exact predicates where possible.
  • Handle degenerate inputs (collinear, duplicate points) explicitly in algorithms and tests.
  • Visualizing geometric algorithms often reveals subtle bugs—use plotting as a debugging aid.
Resources
  • Computational geometry textbooks, Shewchuk’s lectures on robust predicates, and open-source geometry libraries for reference implementations.
